1:
On Luke 12:1 — “In the mean time… he began to say unto his disciples first of all, Beware ye of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy” — the witnesses mark the gathering crowd and the warning that opens the discourse.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Barnes and Ellicott note the original is “myriads” — “ten thousands” — a vast throng pressing so close they “trod one upon another”; the dispute begun in the Pharisee’s house (Luke 11:53) had drawn the multitude. Lightfoot reads the “innumerable” as the fruit of the seventy disciples’ preaching. Robertson notes the classic idiom “in the meantime” (en hois), and that the warning against the Pharisees’ “leaven” is spoken “to his disciples first” but in the people’s hearing.

The Eastern Theophylact draws the irony: the Pharisees “sought to catch Jesus… that they might lead away the people… But this design… is reversed,” for the crowds press all the more to him — “so mighty a thing is truth, so feeble… deceit.” The Latin Tertullian, against Marcion, marks that the hypocrisy displeasing to Christ was loving God “with their lips, but not with their heart” — the Son hating those who refuse obedience to the Father.

So the witnesses set the great discourse in its frame. Amid a numberless crowd, with the Pharisees’ enmity fresh, the Lord turns first to his disciples with a warning against the one peril that had ruined their adversaries: hypocrisy, the “leaven” that works secretly and spreads. The Puritan Henry notes that the people, “not under the bias of” the scribes’ jealousies, “still admired him.” The verse thus opens a teaching on sincerity, courage, and trust — beginning where all true religion must, with the inward heart laid bare before God, and the secret hypocrisy that the day of judgement will expose.

So the witnesses set the keynote of the discourse. Theophylact marks how the Pharisees’ plotting recoiled — the people thronged the more, “so mighty a thing is truth, so feeble… deceit” — and against that backdrop the Lord names the one poison that had destroyed his adversaries: hypocrisy, the “leaven” that works in secret and spreads through the whole. He warns his disciples “first,” for they were most in danger of catching it; and the very crowding of the myriads, eager and sincere, rebukes the hollow religion of the few. The witnesses thus open a teaching on inward truth before God: sincerity, courage in confession, and trust — all of which begin where the warning begins, with a heart that is in reality what it professes to be. The witnesses press the abiding application: the disciple of Christ must guard his heart against the secret beginnings of pretence, for hypocrisy, once admitted, spreads silently through the whole soul, and what starts as a small insincerity ends in a religion wholly hollow — a counterfeit that, like leaven, has soured the entire lump of a man's profession.
2:
On Luke 12:2 — “For there is nothing covered, that shall not be revealed; neither hid, that shall not be known” — the witnesses read the certain disclosure of all that is secret.

The grammatical-historical commentators link it to the warning against hypocrisy and to its parallel in Matthew 10:26. Ellicott corrects the conjunction — better “but” than “for”: “Beware ye of… hypocrisy; but, whether ye beware or not, know that all that is now secret will one day be manifested.” Robertson notes the strong compound “covered up” (synkekalymmenon, completely concealed), and that these verses are “parallel with” the charge to the Twelve in Matthew. The Reformed Gill applies it first to hypocrisy — “nothing more covered than hypocrisy” — which shall be “detected sooner or later; if not in this world… yet at the last judgment.”

The verse drives the warning home with a solemn certainty. Hypocrisy is, of all sins, the most carefully hidden — a show of godliness over an unsurrendered heart; and the Lord declares that no such concealment can last. What is masked from men is open to God, and what is now secret will one day be made wholly manifest.

So the witnesses find here both a terror and a goad to sincerity. The terror: every hidden thing — every secret sin, every cloaked motive, every masked unbelief — will be uncovered at the judgement of God, before whom nothing is concealed. The goad: since all must come to light, the only wisdom is to be in truth what we profess to be, to have no inner life we would dread to see revealed. The verse thus seals the warning against the Pharisees’ leaven: hypocrisy is not only wicked but futile, a covering that the day of God will strip away, leaving the heart bare before the eternal Judge.

So the witnesses seal the warning with the certainty of disclosure. Robertson notes the strong compound — “covered up” on all sides, completely — and that the day comes when the most careful concealment fails. Gill applies it pointedly to hypocrisy, “than which nothing is more covered,” yet which shall be “detected sooner or later.” The terror and the goad lie together: terror, that every secret sin and masked motive will be uncovered before God; goad, that since all must come to light, the only wisdom is to be in truth what we profess. The witnesses thus read the verse as the death-blow to the Pharisees’ leaven — hypocrisy is not only wicked but futile, a covering the day of God will strip away, leaving the heart bare before the Judge from whom nothing is hid. The expositors note the searching reach of this truth: it touches not the gross sins only but the most hidden falseness of the heart, the inward divergence between profession and reality that no eye but God's can see; and the certainty that even this shall be revealed leaves the hypocrite no refuge, but presses every soul to be, in the secret place, what it professes to be in the open.
3:
On Luke 12:3 — “whatsoever ye have spoken in darkness shall be heard in the light; and that which ye have spoken in the ear… shall be proclaimed upon the housetops” — the witnesses press the disclosure to its furthest reach.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the imagery and the variation from Matthew. Barnes explains the “housetops”: “the custom of making proclamation from the tops or roofs of houses still prevails in the East,” local governors publishing their commands so. Ellicott marks the difference of bearing: where Matthew 10:27 bids the disciples publish what Christ tells them in secret, here they are warned of their responsibility — what they have spoken privately shall be made public. Robertson notes the “inner chambers” (tameiois), the secret store-rooms.

The verse extends the law of disclosure to every private word. Nothing whispered in darkness or breathed in the ear is beyond the reach of that final publishing; the secret counsel will be cried from the housetops. The hypocrite’s hidden speech, no less than his hidden heart, will be brought into the open.

So the witnesses gather the threefold warning of these verses into one searching truth: that we live every moment before a God to whom darkness and light are both alike, and that the day comes when the most private word will be as public as a proclamation from the rooftop. The lesson is not dread alone but candour: to speak in secret nothing we would not own in the light, to let our hidden words match our open profession. The leaven of the Pharisees — the double life of one thing said abroad and another in the ear — cannot survive the day when all that is whispered shall be shouted, and every covered thing revealed.

So the witnesses extend the law of disclosure to every private word. Barnes pictures the Eastern custom — proclamation made “from the tops or roofs of houses” — so that what was whispered in the ear shall be cried abroad. The lesson is candour: to speak in secret nothing we would not own in the light, to let our hidden words match our open profession. The double life of the Pharisee — one thing said abroad, another in the chamber — cannot survive the day when all that is covered is revealed. The witnesses thus gather the threefold warning into one searching truth: we live every moment before a God to whom darkness and light are alike, and the most private word will one day be as public as a herald’s cry from the rooftop. The witnesses draw the practical warning: since the day of full disclosure is certain, the only safety lies in a transparent sincerity that would not be ashamed to have its most private words and motives cried from the housetops — for what a man would hide is precisely what the coming light will most surely expose.
4:
On Luke 12:4 — “And I say unto you my friends, Be not afraid of them that kill the body, and after that have no more that they can do” — the witnesses hear the Lord arm his friends against the fear of man.

The Latin and Eastern fathers mark the address and the comfort. Ambrose: since unbelief springs “either from a deeply-seated malice or a sudden fear,” the Lord adds this “lest any one from terror should be compelled to deny the God whom he acknowledges in his heart.” Cyril sees “crowns and honours… prepared” for those upon whom men vent their rage, “to them the death of the body is the end of their persecutions”; Bede adds that the persecutors’ rage is “useless raving,” able to touch only the lifeless limbs. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes “my friends” set against the Pharisees and lawyers (Luke 11:53), and the ingressive aorist — “do not become afraid.”

The Reformed Gill dwells on the tenderness of “my friends” — those whom Christ “dearly loved… and for whom he after laid down his life” (John 15:14). Wesley draws the point: “Let not the fear of man make you act the hypocrite, or conceal any thing which I have commissioned you to publish.”

So the witnesses join this word to the warning against hypocrisy. The leaven of the Pharisees is bred partly by the fear of man, which tempts the believer to hide or deny his faith; against it the Lord sets the limit of all human malice — it can kill the body, and there it stops. Persecutors have no power beyond the grave; the death they inflict is, for the friend of Christ, but the end of his sufferings. The verse thus steels the confessor: he need not purchase safety by hypocrisy, for the worst that men can do reaches only the body, and is met by One who calls his suffering people his “friends.”

So the witnesses join this word to the warning against hypocrisy. The leaven of the Pharisees is bred partly by the fear of man, which tempts the believer to hide or deny his faith; against it the Lord sets the limit of all human malice — it can kill the body, and there it stops. Ambrose marks that the comfort guards “lest any one from terror should be compelled to deny the God whom he acknowledges in his heart”; Cyril, that to the persecuted “the death of the body is the end of their persecutions.” The witnesses thus steel the confessor: he need not purchase safety by hypocrisy, for the worst that men can do reaches only the body, and is met by One who calls his suffering people his “friends,” loved and died for. The expositors press the encouragement for every age of persecution: the friend of Christ, weighing the brief and bounded power of his persecutors against the eternal weight of glory, may meet even the threat of death with a holy serenity, knowing that the worst men can do reaches no further than the body, while the soul and its everlasting hope rest secure in the hand of God.
5:
On Luke 12:5 — “Fear him, which after he hath killed hath power to cast into hell; yea, I say unto you, Fear him” — the witnesses mark the one fear that drives out the fear of man.

The Reformed Calvin reads the emphatic repetition: “when we give way to the dread of men, we pay no respect to God; and… if on the contrary we fear God, we have an easy victory… so that no efforts of men will draw us aside.” The fear of God, rightly placed, is the cure for the fear of man. The Wesleyan-Arminian voices press that this fear belongs even to believers: Clarke, “even the friends of God are commanded to fear God… therefore it is proper even for the most holy persons”; Wesley, “the fearing of God as having power to cast into hell is to be pressed even on true believers.”

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the deliberative subjunctive retained in the indirect question, and the force of the doubled command; Clarke adds the sobering arithmetic — “a man has but one life to lose, and one soul to save; and it is madness to sacrifice the salvation of the soul to the preservation of the life.”

So the witnesses set the two fears in their true proportion. Men can reach the body; God holds body and soul, with power over the second death. To fear the lesser power, that can only kill, and not the greater, that can cast into hell, is folly; and the surest deliverance from the dread of persecutors is a deeper dread of God. The witnesses are careful, with Calvin and Wesley, to press this even on the godly: it is not a slavish terror that quenches love, but a holy awe of the God who is to be obeyed above all men — and from it springs the courage that no threat of death can break.

So the witnesses set the two fears in their true proportion. Men can reach the body; God holds body and soul, with power over the second death. Calvin draws the cure: “if… we fear God, we have an easy victory… so that no efforts of men will draw us aside.” Clarke and Wesley press that this awe belongs even to believers and the most holy — not a slavish terror that quenches love, but a holy dread of the God to be obeyed above all men. The witnesses thus find the deliverance from the fear of man in a deeper fear of God: to dread the lesser power that only kills, and not the greater that can cast into hell, is folly; and from the right fear of God springs the courage that no threat of death can break. The witnesses note that this fear of God is no servile dread that drives the soul from him, but the reverent awe that keeps it near; for he who truly fears the God who holds his eternal destiny will find in that fear his surest defence against every lesser fear, standing unmoved before the threats of men because he trembles rightly before the Lord of heaven and hell.
6:
On Luke 12:6 — “Are not five sparrows sold for two farthings, and not one of them is forgotten before God?” — the witnesses read the comfort of providence in the least of creatures.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the vivid market-detail. Ellicott marks the variation from Matthew 10:29 (“two sparrows… a farthing”): here five for two farthings — “the sparrow was of so little value that the odd bird was thrown in to tempt the purchasers,” reproducing “the very bargains of the market-place.” Lightfoot supposes the sparrows sold for the lepers’ offering. Robertson notes the periphrastic perfect “is forgotten” (epilelesmenon), and Barnes the difference from Matthew’s “not one shall fall… without your Father.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley draws the balance with the foregoing fear: “trust as well as fear him.” The same God who is to be feared as the Judge of souls is to be trusted as the Father who forgets not a sparrow.

So the witnesses join awe and assurance. The sparrow, the cheapest thing in the market — five for two farthings, the odd one thrown in — is not forgotten before God; how much less his persecuted friends. The argument runs from the least to the greatest: if the providence of God reaches to the worthless bird, it cannot fail the disciple who suffers for his name. The verse thus tempers the fear of God with the trust of a child: the God before whom we are to tremble as Judge is the same who numbers the sparrows and forgets none of them, and who therefore may be relied upon to hold his own through every danger that the fear of man would magnify.

So the witnesses join awe and assurance. The sparrow, the cheapest thing in the market — five for two farthings, the odd one thrown in — is not forgotten before God; how much less his persecuted friends. The argument runs from the least to the greatest: if providence reaches to the worthless bird, it cannot fail the disciple who suffers for the name. Wesley draws the balance with the foregoing dread — “trust as well as fear him” — for the God before whom we tremble as Judge is the same who numbers the sparrows and forgets none. The witnesses thus temper the fear of God with the trust of a child: the very God who holds the power of hell stoops to remember a sparrow, and may therefore be relied on to hold his own through every danger. The expositors press the tenderness of the lesson: the providence that stoops to the sparrow is the same that watches every step of the believer's path, so that nothing — not persecution, not loss, not death itself — befalls him unnoticed or uncared-for by the God who reckons even the fall of the cheapest bird.
7:
On Luke 12:7 — “But even the very hairs of your head are all numbered. Fear not therefore: ye are of more value than many sparrows” — the witnesses press the providence of God to its tenderest point.

The Reformed Gill marvels at the reach of it: not only the persons and members of the disciples, but “the more minute parts” — the very hairs — are noted by “the wonderful numberer.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke turns it on unbelief: “want of faith in the providence and goodness of God is the source of all human inquietudes and fears”; man distrusts God “because he judges of God by himself.” The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the perfect passive “numbered” (erithmentai), a settled state — the hairs stand counted.

The verse crowns the argument from the sparrows. If God forgets not the cheapest bird, and if he has numbered the very hairs of his children’s heads — a thing past all our reckoning — then nothing that touches them is beneath his notice or beyond his care. “Fear not therefore”: the conclusion is drawn from the greater to the less, the disciple far outweighing many sparrows.

So the witnesses gather the comfort that disarms the fear of man. The God to be feared as the Judge of souls is the same who counts the hairs of his people’s heads; his providence is not general only but minute, reaching to the smallest particular of their lives. The root of all anxious fear, the witnesses observe with Clarke, is the failure to believe this — the suspicion that God may forget us as we forget him. The verse answers that suspicion with the most intimate assurance: we are of great value to our Father, watched over to the last hair, and may therefore meet the worst that men can do without dismay.

So the witnesses crown the argument from the sparrows. Gill marvels that even “the more minute parts” — the very hairs — are noted by “the wonderful numberer”; and Clarke finds in the failure to believe this “the source of all human inquietudes and fears.” If God forgets not the cheapest bird, and has numbered the hairs of his children’s heads — a thing past all our reckoning — then nothing that touches them is beneath his notice. “Fear not therefore”: the disciple far outweighs many sparrows. The witnesses thus answer the root of all anxious fear — the suspicion that God may forget us as we forget him — with the most intimate assurance: we are watched over to the last hair, of great value to our Father, and may meet the worst that men can do without dismay. The witnesses draw the abiding comfort: the believer, assured that he is known to the last hair and kept by a providence so particular, may lay down every fear, confident that the God who counts what is least about him will not fail to guard what is greatest — his soul, his salvation, and his eternal welfare.
8:
On Luke 12:8 — “Whosoever shall confess me before men, him shall the Son of man also confess before the angels of God” — the witnesses mark the call to open confession and its great reward.

The Eastern and Latin fathers press the necessity of confession. Chrysostom: “the Lord is not… content with an inward faith, but requires an outward confession.” Cyril cites Paul — “if thou shalt confess with thy mouth… thou shalt be saved” (Romans 10:9) — and finds “the whole mystery of Christ” in it. Ambrose places faith as the foundation and confession as its fruit. Eusebius marvels at the recompense: “what will be more glorious than to have the only-begotten Word… bear witness in our behalf at the divine judgment.” The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott note the parallel in Matthew 10:32, and the variation — “before the angels of God” where Matthew has “before my Father.”

The verse joins the foregoing courage to its open expression. Having armed his friends against the fear of man, the Lord now requires the confession that fear would silence — and crowns it with a promise: the disciple who owns Christ before men shall be owned by Christ before the angels of God. The witness borne below is answered by a witness borne above.

So the witnesses set faith and confession together. An inward faith that dares not confess is not the faith the gospel requires; the heart that believes must find a mouth that owns (Romans 10:9–10). And the reward is past all telling: that the Son of man himself should name us before the angels, drawing forth, as Eusebius says, “a declaration upon that soul to whom He bears witness.” The verse thus presses the confessor onward through every danger — for the worst that men can do is to kill the body, but to be confessed by Christ before the angels of God is a glory that outweighs all loss.

So the witnesses set faith and confession together. Chrysostom: “the Lord is not… content with an inward faith, but requires an outward confession”; Cyril finds “the whole mystery of Christ” in Paul’s “confess with thy mouth… and believe in thy heart” (Romans 10:9). An inward faith that dares not confess is not the faith the gospel requires; and the reward is past telling — that the Son of man should own us “before the angels of God.” Eusebius marvels at the recompense: the only-begotten Word himself bearing witness for us at the divine judgment. The witnesses thus press the confessor onward through every danger: the worst men can do is kill the body, but to be confessed by Christ before the angels is a glory that outweighs all loss. The expositors press the encouragement to the timid: the momentary cost of owning Christ before a scoffing world is repaid beyond all measure by the eternal honour of being owned by Christ before the assembled host of heaven, so that the faithful confessor loses nothing and gains all by his bold acknowledgment of his Lord.
9:
On Luke 12:9 — “But he that denieth me before men shall be denied before the angels of God” — the witnesses mark the dark counterpart of the promise.

The voices here are few but weighty. The Reformed Gill guards the sense against despair: it means “that continues to deny Christ, and lives and dies a denier of him” — for “through temptation, a real Christian may be left for a while… to deny him… and afterwards truly repent,” as Peter did. Lightfoot weighs whether this verse and the next point to “those two unpardonable sins, apostasy… and blasphemy… against the Holy Ghost.” The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the compound “shall be denied” (aparnethesetai), stronger than Matthew’s simple form, and the variation “before the angels of God.”

The verse sets the solemn alternative to confession. As the one who owns Christ is owned, so the one who denies him is disowned — and that before the angels of God, at the last great assize. The fear of man that silences confession, if it hardens into a settled denial, ends in being denied by Christ himself.

So the witnesses hold the promise and the warning together, and guard the warning from misuse. It is not the single failure under sudden terror, repented of like Peter’s, that this verse threatens, but the persistent, final denial of one who will not own Christ at all. Yet the warning is real and searching: the confession the gospel requires is not optional, and to be ashamed of Christ before men is to risk his being ashamed of us before the angels. The verse thus presses the disciple to a courageous and lifelong owning of his Lord, knowing that the mouth’s confession or denial here is answered by Christ’s acknowledgement or refusal hereafter.

So the witnesses hold the promise and its dark counterpart together, and guard the warning from misuse. Gill is careful: it threatens not the single lapse under sudden terror, repented of like Peter’s, but the settled, final denial of one who “lives and dies a denier”; Lightfoot links it to apostasy, “a sin unto death.” Yet the warning is real and searching: the confession the gospel requires is not optional, and to be ashamed of Christ before men is to risk his being ashamed of us before the angels. The witnesses thus press a courageous, lifelong owning of the Lord. The mouth’s confession or denial here is answered by Christ’s acknowledgement or refusal hereafter — a solemn alternative set before every disciple tempted to silence by the fear of man. The witnesses note the searching application: every professor of Christ is here summoned to hold fast his confession through trial and fear, while the penitent who has stumbled may take heart that his fall, if followed by true repentance, is not the final denial that Christ disowns, but a wound that grace can heal, as it healed the weeping Peter.
10:
On Luke 12:10 — “Whosoever shall speak a word against the Son of man, it shall be forgiven him: but unto him that blasphemeth against the Holy Ghost it shall not be forgiven” — the witnesses grapple with the unpardonable sin.

The Latin and Eastern fathers wrestle with the difficulty. Ambrose meets the objection that the Spirit would seem greater than Christ, since sin against the one is pardonable and against the other not: “where there is unity of power there is no question of comparison.” Athanasius reports the ancients — Origen and Theognostus — who defined the blasphemy as the sin of those “counted worthy of the gift of the Holy Spirit in Baptism” who “fall back into sin” (cf. Hebrews 6:4). The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott set it in its original context — the charge that Jesus cast out devils by Beelzebub (Matthew 12:31) — Luke here repeating it “in its more general bearing.”

The Reformed Lightfoot distinguishes the “two unpardonable sins” — apostasy and the blasphemy — and Wesley explains the gradation: “the denying me in some degree may, upon true repentance, be forgiven; but if it rise so high as… the blasphemy against the Holy Ghost, it shall never be forgiven.”

So the witnesses, for all their differences over the precise definition, agree on the substance. A word against the Son of man — spoken in ignorance, against his lowly humanity — may be forgiven; but the blasphemy against the Holy Ghost, the wilful and malicious rejection of the Spirit’s plain testimony to Christ (as when his works were ascribed to Satan), is not forgiven, because it is the settled refusal of the only power that brings men to repentance. The witnesses are careful to fence the saying from the despair of the tender conscience: it describes not a hasty word repented of, but a hardened, final resistance to the Spirit of God.

So the witnesses, for all their difference over the precise definition, agree on the substance. A word against the Son of man — spoken in ignorance, against his lowly humanity — may be forgiven; but the blasphemy against the Holy Ghost, the wilful and malicious rejection of the Spirit’s plain testimony to Christ (as when his works were ascribed to Satan), is not forgiven, because it is the settled refusal of the only power that brings men to repentance. Ambrose answers the objection that the Spirit seems thereby greater than Christ — “where there is unity of power there is no question of comparison.” The witnesses are careful to fence the saying from the despair of the tender conscience: it describes not a hasty word repented of, but a hardened, final resistance to the Spirit of God, in which the door of repentance is itself shut. The expositors press the pastoral counsel often drawn from this text: the very anxiety of a troubled soul that it has committed the unpardonable sin is itself evidence that it has not, for the hardness that truly blasphemes the Spirit feels no such fear, while the heart that grieves and seeks forgiveness shows the Spirit still striving with it, and may freely come to the mercy that is denied only to the finally hardened.
11:
On Luke 12:11 — “And when they bring you unto the synagogues, and unto magistrates, and powers, take ye no thought how or what thing ye shall answer” — the witnesses read the promise of help under persecution.

The grammatical-historical commentators set it in its context. Ellicott notes that “what had been a special promise to the Twelve” (Matthew 10:18) “is now extended to all whom the Lord calls His friends,” and marks the Lukan combination “magistrates… and powers” (better, “authorities”). Robertson reads “be not anxious” (me merimnesete, do not become anxious), the same charge given on the Galilean tour and in the Olivet discourse (Mark 13:11). The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke and Wesley: “take no thought” — “be not solicitous about the matter or manner of your defence.”

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem fastens on the deeper truth: “by the power of the Holy Ghost the Martyrs bear their witness,” for “it is impossible to testify as a martyr… except by the Holy Ghost.”

So the witnesses gather the comfort for the hour of trial. The friends of Christ, dragged before synagogue and magistrate, are not to be tormented beforehand with anxious rehearsal of their defence; the Spirit who teaches will supply, in the moment, both matter and words. The witnesses are careful (with Robertson on the next verse) that this is “no excuse for neglect” in ordinary preparation, but a promise of special help in a sudden crisis. The same God who numbers the hairs and forbids the fear of man here pledges, to those brought before the powers for his name, the immediate aid of the Holy Ghost — that they may play the man for Christ and leave the issue with God.

So the witnesses gather the comfort for the hour of trial. The friends of Christ, dragged before synagogue and magistrate, are not to be tormented beforehand with anxious rehearsal; the Spirit who teaches will supply, in the moment, both matter and words. Ellicott notes the promise once special to the Twelve is now extended to all his friends; Cyril of Jerusalem, that “it is impossible to testify as a martyr… except by the Holy Ghost.” The witnesses are careful that this is no excuse for neglect in ordinary preparation, but a pledge of special help in a sudden crisis. The same God who numbers the hairs and forbids the fear of man here promises, to those brought before the powers for his name, the immediate aid of the Spirit — that they may play the man and leave the issue with God. The witnesses draw the abiding comfort for the suffering Church: in every age when the followers of Christ are dragged before hostile courts, this word stands — that they need not be paralyzed by anxious forethought, for their cause does not rest on their own eloquence but on the God who has promised to stand with his witnesses in the hour of their trial.
12:
On Luke 12:12 — “For the Holy Ghost shall teach you in the same hour what ye ought to say” — the witnesses mark the ground of the foregoing assurance.

The voices here are brief and pointed. The Reformed Gill: the Spirit “shall give both words and matter… in the same hour; instantly, immediately.” The grammatical-historical Robertson presses the guard against abuse: this “is no excuse for neglect in pulpit preparation… It is simply a word for courage in a crisis to play the man for Christ and to trust the issue with God without fear.” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem repeats that “the Holy Ghost shall teach you in that very hour,” for the martyr’s testimony is borne “by the power of the Holy Ghost.”

The verse completes the promise of the last. The reason the confessor need take no anxious thought is that, in the very hour of his trial, the Holy Ghost will teach him what to say. The defence of Christ’s friends before the powers is not finally their own work but the Spirit’s, who supplies in the moment both the words and the wisdom.

So the witnesses set the seal on the whole passage of courage. The fear of man is answered not only by the fear of God and the assurance of his providence, but by the present help of his Spirit. The disciple brought before the tribunal is not left to his own resources of eloquence or memory; he is promised a Teacher in the hour of need. The witnesses balance this finely — it is no warrant for laziness in ordinary study, but a pledge of extraordinary aid in extraordinary crisis — so that the confessor may go to the synagogue and the magistrate without dread, trusting the Spirit to make him, in that hour, a faithful witness for his Lord.

So the witnesses set the seal on the whole passage of courage. The reason the confessor need take no anxious thought is that, in the very hour of his trial, the Holy Ghost will teach him what to say. Gill: the Spirit gives “both words and matter… instantly, immediately”; Robertson guards it — “no excuse for neglect in… preparation,” but “courage in a crisis to play the man for Christ.” The disciple before the tribunal is not left to his own eloquence or memory but promised a Teacher in the hour of need. The witnesses thus complete the cure for the fear of man: it is answered by the fear of God, the assurance of his providence, and now the present help of his Spirit — so that the friend of Christ may face synagogue and magistrate without dread, trusting the Spirit to make him a faithful witness. The expositors press the balance the text requires: the promise of the Spirit's help in the crisis of persecution neither excuses the preacher's diligence in ordinary study nor leaves the martyr to his own strength, but assures the confessor that, when human resources fail before the tribunal, the Spirit of God will supply both the courage and the words, so that the testimony borne is in truth the Spirit's own.
13:
On Luke 12:13 — “And one of the company said unto him, Master, speak to my brother, that he divide the inheritance with me” — the witnesses read the interruption that occasions the teaching on covetousness.

The Latin and Eastern fathers mark the Lord’s refusal of the office. Ambrose: “well does He avoid earthly things who had descended for the sake of divine things,” deigning not to be “a judge of strifes… having the judgment of the quick and dead.” Bede: he “takes occasion from this foolish petitioner to fortify both the multitudes and His disciples… against the plague of covetousness.” The grammatical-historical commentators set the legal background: Robertson notes the law gave “two-thirds to the elder, one-third to the younger” (Deuteronomy 21:17), and that the man “wants a decision… against his brother,” not arbitration; Ellicott, that the scribes were appealed to “as advocates and arbitrators in questions of property.”

The Reformed Calvin asks why the Lord, whose office was to bring men into agreement, declined the dispute — and answers that he would keep to his spiritual calling and not be entangled in the secular.

So the witnesses see in the interruption both a man’s covetousness and the Lord’s wisdom. One of the crowd, hearing the great words of confession and providence, can think only of his own purse, and would make Christ the arbiter of his estate. The Lord declines — not because justice is beneath him, but because he came for a higher work, and will not let the kingdom of God be turned into a court of property. The verse thus opens, by way of a sad example, the teaching against covetousness: the man who heard of heaven and hell and the angels of God was concerned only that his brother should divide the land.

So the witnesses see in the interruption both a man’s covetousness and the Lord’s wisdom. One of the crowd, hearing the great words of confession and providence, can think only of his purse, and would make Christ the arbiter of his estate. Ambrose marks that the Lord, “who had descended for the sake of divine things,” deigns not to be “a judge of strifes”; Bede, that he “takes occasion from this foolish petitioner to fortify… against the plague of covetousness.” The Lord declines — not because justice is beneath him, but because he came for a higher work and will not turn the kingdom of God into a court of property. The witnesses thus read a sad example opening the teaching against covetousness: the man who heard of heaven and hell cared only that his brother divide the land. The witnesses note the searching mirror this holds to every hearer: how often the heart, even while the things of God are pressed upon it, runs back to its own earthly interest — its money, its rights, its share — proving by that very wandering how deeply covetousness is rooted, and how needful is the warning Christ is about to give.
14:
On Luke 12:14 — “And he said unto him, Man, who made me a judge or a divider over you?” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s firm refusal of the secular office.

The grammatical-historical commentators read both the refusal and its tone. Barnes: “it is not my business to settle controversies of this kind… Jesus came… to preach the gospel… Civil affairs are to be left to the magistrate.” Robertson hears an echo of Exodus 2:14 (“who made thee a ruler and a judge?”), and that Jesus “renders unto Caesar the things of Caesar” (Luke 20:25), his kingdom “not of this world” (John 18:36); the rare word “divider” (meristes) occurs here only. Ellicott notes the grave address “Man” (cf. Romans 2:1), expressing censure — was it for this that men came to him?

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke draws the ministerial lesson: “a minister of Christ ought not to concern himself with secular affairs… The example of a perfect disengagement from worldly things was more necessary for the” ministry.

So the witnesses find in the refusal a deliberate self-limitation. Christ could have settled the dispute in a moment; that he would not is itself instruction. His kingdom is not of this world, and he will not be diverted from the gospel into the arbitration of estates. The grave “Man” carries a rebuke: the petitioner had heard the things of God and asked only for the things of earth. The witnesses thus read a pattern for Christ’s ministers — to keep free of entanglement in secular business, that they may give themselves to the spiritual work for which, like their Master, they are sent.

So the witnesses find in the refusal a deliberate self-limitation. Christ could have settled the dispute in a moment; that he would not is itself instruction. Robertson hears the echo of Exodus 2:14 and the principle that his kingdom is “not of this world” (John 18:36); Ellicott, that the grave address “Man” carries censure — was it for this men came to him? Clarke draws the ministerial lesson: “a minister of Christ ought not to concern himself with secular affairs… The example of… disengagement from worldly things was… necessary.” The witnesses thus read a pattern for Christ’s servants: to keep free of entanglement in secular business, that they may give themselves, like their Master, to the spiritual work for which they are sent, and not let the things of earth crowd out the things of God. The expositors draw the lesson for the Church's ministers, who, like their Master, are to keep to the cure of souls and the preaching of the gospel, not entangling themselves as arbiters in the temporal disputes of men; for the dividing of inheritances belongs to the magistrate, but the dividing asunder of soul and spirit, and the turning of the heart from covetousness to God, is the proper and weightier work of Christ's servants.
15:
On Luke 12:15 — “Take heed, and beware of covetousness: for a man’s life consisteth not in the abundance of the things which he possesseth” — the witnesses mark the warning and its root.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the breadth and the definition. Ellicott and Clarke note the better text reads “all” (every form of) covetousness, and that the Greek (pleonexia) is “the desire to have more and more”; Barnes calls it “a violation of the tenth commandment” (Exodus 20:17), “expressly called idolatry” (Colossians 3:5). Christ’s words, Ellicott adds, “show that He had read the secret of the man’s heart.”

The Reformed Calvin lays bare the “inward fountain”: covetousness flows from the false belief “that a man is happy in proportion as he possesses much, and that the happiness of life is produced by riches”; Christ cures the disease at its source by denying the premise. Wesley: “a man’s life” means “the comfort or happiness of it.”

So the witnesses gather the great maxim that governs the parable to follow. Life — its true worth and happiness — does not consist in abundance of possessions; and to think it does is the lie at the heart of covetousness. The Lord, having read the petitioner’s greedy heart, warns against “all” covetousness, in every disguise, as a form of idolatry that sets the creature in God’s place. The witnesses mark that this is no mere caution against gross avarice but a cure aimed at the root — the soul’s mistaken faith that more having means more living, which the rich fool will presently expose as folly before God.

So the witnesses gather the great maxim that governs the parable to follow. Life — its true worth and happiness — does not consist in abundance of possessions; and to think it does is the lie at the heart of covetousness. Ellicott notes the better text, “all” covetousness, in every disguise; Barnes, that it is idolatry (Colossians 3:5); Calvin, that it flows from the belief “that a man is happy in proportion as he possesses much.” The Lord, having read the petitioner’s greedy heart, cures the disease at its root by denying its premise. The witnesses mark that this is no mere caution against gross avarice but a warning aimed at every soul: to set the heart on having, as though more goods meant more life, is the folly the rich fool will presently expose before God. The witnesses press the abiding warning: covetousness is the more dangerous for being so respectable a sin, cloaking itself as prudence and diligence, yet it is at bottom idolatry, the heart's trust transferred from God to gold; and against it Christ sets the great truth that no abundance of possessions can secure the one thing every man most needs — life, and the life that is life indeed.
16:
On Luke 12:16 — “The ground of a certain rich man brought forth plentifully” — the witnesses open the parable of the rich fool.

The Eastern fathers press the source of the plenty. Basil: the harvest came “not… about to reap any good… but that the mercy of God might the more appear, which extends… even to the bad,” sending rain on just and unjust — yet the man “remembered not his fellow-creatures, nor deemed that he ought to give of his superfluities to the needy.” Cyril marks his folly in saying “my fruits, as if he thought… they were the fruits of his own labours,” not gifts of God. Basil presses the question: “Why do you abound while another begs?”

The Reformed Calvin reads the parable as “a lively portrait” of the universal sentiment that life lies in possessions: “all acknowledge it to be true… but where is the man that honestly believes it?” The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the rare medical word “brought forth plentifully” (euphoresen).

So the witnesses set the stage of the warning by example. The rich man’s ground yields abundantly — a mercy of God, the fathers insist, not the mere reward of his own toil — and at once his abundance becomes his snare. The parable, the witnesses observe, holds up a mirror: the very sentiment Christ has just denied, that life consists in possessions, is about to be acted out and judged. Basil’s searching questions frame it — the plenty was given that the man might be merciful; the test of riches is what we do with the superfluity God entrusts; and the fool is already failing the test in calling “my fruits” what God had made to grow.

So the witnesses set the stage of the warning by example. The rich man’s ground yields abundantly — a mercy of God, the fathers insist, not the mere reward of his own toil. Basil: the harvest came “that the mercy of God might the more appear” and that he might “give of his superfluities to the needy” — yet he “remembered not his fellow-creatures”; Cyril marks his folly in saying “my fruits.” The parable, the witnesses observe, holds up a mirror: the very sentiment Christ has just denied, that life consists in possessions, is about to be acted out and judged. Basil’s searching question frames it — “why do you abound while another begs?” — for the test of riches is what we do with the superfluity God entrusts, and the fool is failing it already. The expositors draw the lesson that God's gifts are given in trust, to be shared and returned in thankful stewardship; and the rich man's silent hoarding, his forgetfulness of the Giver and of the poor at his gate, is the very sin that turns a blessing into a snare, so that his overflowing barns become the instruments of his ruin rather than the means of his good.
17:
On Luke 12:17 — “And he thought within himself, saying, What shall I do, because I have no room where to bestow my fruits?” — the witnesses mark the anxious soliloquy of the rich fool.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the self-enclosed reasoning. Barnes: “he reasoned… he was anxious and perplexed. Riches increase thought and perplexity… to engross the thoughts and steal the heart away from better things.” Robertson reads the imperfect “reasoned within himself,” picturing his “continued cogitations,” and the repeated “my” — “my fruits, my barns, my corn, my goods, just like Nabal” (1 Samuel 25:11), whose name means fool. Clarke: riches, “ever so well acquired, produce nothing but vexation and embarrassment.”

The Reformed Calvin marks the deeper folly: wicked men are “driven to perplexity… because they do not know how any thing is to be lawfully used,” and are “intoxicated with a foolish confidence which makes them forget themselves.” Wesley hears in “What shall I do?” “the very language of want!” — the irony that abundance leaves him as anxious as poverty.

So the witnesses expose the heart of the man in his own words. His plenty brings not peace but perplexity; his soliloquy is all “I” and “my,” with never a thought of God or of the poor. The very superfluity that should have made him glad and generous makes him anxious and self-absorbed. The witnesses note the bitter irony Wesley fastens on: the rich man, overflowing with goods, asks the question of a beggar, “What shall I do?” — for riches engross the thoughts and steal the heart, and the man who lives for them, however full his barns, is never at rest.

So the witnesses expose the heart of the man in his own words. His plenty brings not peace but perplexity; his soliloquy is all “I” and “my,” with never a thought of God or of the poor. Robertson notes the repeated possessives — “my fruits, my barns, my corn, my goods, just like Nabal” (1 Samuel 25:11); Barnes, that “riches increase thought and perplexity… to engross the thoughts and steal the heart.” The witnesses fasten on the irony Wesley names: the rich man, overflowing with goods, asks the question of a beggar — “What shall I do?” The very superfluity that should have made him glad and generous makes him anxious and self-absorbed, proving that the man who lives for riches, however full his barns, is never at rest. The witnesses press the searching portrait: here is the covetous heart laid bare — shut up within itself, blind to God above and the neighbour beside and the soul within, and finding in its very abundance not rest but a new and gnawing care; so that the man who seemed most prosperous was in truth most impoverished, anxious amid his plenty and destitute of all that truly enriches.
18:
On Luke 12:18 — “This will I do: I will pull down my barns, and build greater; and there will I bestow all my fruits and my goods” — the witnesses mark the resolve taken without God.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the plan. Barnes explains the “barns” as granaries, often underground, and that it would be easier to enlarge than to keep the old; the repeated “my” runs on — “my barns… my fruits… my goods.” Robertson notes the future “I will pull down” (kathelo) and the same drumbeat of possessive pronouns. Clarke: “the rich are full of designs concerning this life, but in general take no thought about eternity till… their goods and their lives are both taken away.” The Reformed Gill marks that “this was the resolution he came to… without consulting God, or asking leave of him.”

The verse shows the fool laying his plans as though he were master of his own future. Every clause is in the first person and the future tense: I will pull down, I will build, there will I bestow. God is nowhere in the reckoning; the poor are nowhere; eternity is nowhere. The whole horizon is bigger barns.

So the witnesses read in the resolve the very essence of practical atheism. The man does not deny God; he simply leaves him out, disposing of his abundance as if it were his own to command and his life his own to prolong. The witnesses mark the contrast the parable presses: there were better barns at hand — the bellies of the poor, the treasury of heaven — but the fool can think only of hoarding. His plan is shrewd by the world’s measure and senseless by God’s, for it provides for many years and forgets the night that is at the door.

So the witnesses read in the resolve the essence of practical atheism. Every clause is in the first person and the future tense — I will pull down, build, bestow — and God is nowhere in the reckoning, the poor nowhere, eternity nowhere; the whole horizon is bigger barns. Gill marks that “this was the resolution he came to… without consulting God”; Clarke, that “the rich are full of designs concerning this life, but… take no thought about eternity.” The witnesses note the contrast the parable presses: there were better barns at hand — the bellies of the poor, the treasury of heaven — but the fool can think only of hoarding. His plan is shrewd by the world’s measure and senseless by God’s, providing for many years and forgetting the night that is at the door. The expositors draw the warning against all such presumption: to dispose of years yet ungiven, to build and enlarge as though life and time lay in our own hands, is the folly James rebukes — for we know not what a day may bring forth, and the wise man holds his plans always subject to the will of God, who alone grants or withholds the morrow.
19:
On Luke 12:19 — “And I will say to my soul, Soul, thou hast much goods laid up for many years; take thine ease, eat, drink, and be merry” — the witnesses mark the fool’s creed of self-indulgence.

The grammatical-historical commentators name the philosophy. Barnes and Clarke: “this was just the doctrine of the ancient Epicureans and atheists” — “eat, drink, and be merry” (cf. 1 Corinthians 15:32, “let us eat and drink, for to-morrow we die”). Robertson notes the apostrophe is “thoroughly Epicurean, for his soul feeds on his goods,” and the eager asyndeton of the commands — rest, eat, drink, be merry. Lightfoot quotes the rabbinic rebuke of just such ease in a time of distress.

The Reformed Calvin marks the swelling pride: he “no longer remembers that he is a man, but swells… by relying on his abundance,” holding up his riches “as a brazen rampart against death.”

So the witnesses expose the soul of the rich fool laid bare in his self-address. He speaks to his soul as if it could be fed on grain and wine, promising it “many years” of ease — the creed of the Epicurean who knows no good beyond the body and no future beyond this life. The witnesses mark the double folly: he reckons on years that are not his to grant, and he offers his immortal soul a banquet of bread and merriment. It is the portrait of a man rich toward himself and toward the world, who has made his goods his god and his belly his hope — and who is, that very night, to be undeceived.

So the witnesses expose the soul of the rich fool laid bare in his self-address. He speaks to his soul as if it could be fed on grain and wine, promising it “many years” of ease — the creed of the Epicurean (Barnes, Clarke), “eat, drink, and be merry” (cf. 1 Corinthians 15:32). Robertson: the apostrophe is “thoroughly Epicurean, for his soul feeds on his goods”; Calvin: he “swells… by relying on his abundance,” holding his riches “as a brazen rampart against death.” The witnesses mark the double folly: he reckons on years not his to grant, and offers his immortal soul a banquet of bread and merriment. It is the portrait of a man rich toward himself and the world, who has made his goods his god and his belly his hope — and is, that very night, to be undeceived. The witnesses press the great folly the verse exposes: to offer an immortal soul the food of beasts, and to ground its rest upon perishing goods, is to mistake utterly both the soul's nature and its need; for the soul made for God cannot be filled with bread and wine, nor find security in barns, but only in the God whom this man had wholly forgotten.
20:
On Luke 12:20 — “But God said unto him, Thou fool, this night thy soul shall be required of thee: then whose shall those things be?” — the witnesses mark the sudden, shattering verdict of God.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the word and the timing. Barnes: “if there is any supreme folly, it is this” — “as though riches could prolong life… In a single moment all his hopes were blasted.” Robertson notes “fool” (aphron, lacking sense) and the impersonal plural — “they are demanding thy soul from thee” (Luke 12:48 the like idiom). Clarke: “how awful was this saying!” in the very night his plans were settled, his soul was summoned. The Reformed Calvin and Wesley read the impersonal “they require” — the life “which he imagined to be in his own power, was at the disposal of another” (Calvin); the messengers of death, “commissioned by God, require thy soul” (Wesley).

The verse turns the parable on its hinge. God, who was nowhere in the fool’s reckoning, now speaks — and his word is death. “This night” — not after the many years he had promised himself, but before the new barns could be built — the soul he had pampered is required of him; and the goods he had hoarded pass to another. The two great certainties he had ignored, God and death, break in together.

So the witnesses read the fool’s undeceiving. He had counted on years and is granted not a day; he had laid up for himself and must leave it all; he had spoken to his soul of ease and hears from God of judgement. The very question — “whose shall those things be?” — exposes the emptiness of all his toil: he had gathered for himself and can keep nothing. The witnesses mark the solemn lesson the next verse will draw: that to live for goods and forget God is to be, in the end, the supreme fool, surprised by a death that strips away the riches and summons the soul to its account.

So the witnesses read the fool’s undeceiving. God, who was nowhere in his reckoning, now speaks — and his word is death. “This night” — not after the many years he promised himself — the soul he had pampered is required of him, and the goods he hoarded pass to another. Robertson notes the impersonal plural, “they require thy soul,” and Calvin that the life “which he imagined to be in his own power, was at the disposal of another.” The very question, “whose shall those things be?”, exposes the emptiness of all his toil. The witnesses mark the lesson the next verse draws: to live for goods and forget God is to be the supreme fool, surprised by a death that strips the riches away and summons the soul to its account. The expositors draw the abiding lesson: death comes at God's appointed hour, often when least expected and least prepared for; and all the wealth a man has gathered cannot purchase one more night of life, but passes to others while he stands stripped before his Judge — so that the only true riches are those laid up with God, which death cannot touch nor strangers inherit.
21:
On Luke 12:21 — “So is he that layeth up treasure for himself, and is not rich toward God” — the witnesses draw the moral of the parable.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the two contrasted phrases. Barnes: the covetous man “lays them up… for himself… His affections are all fixed” on earth, with “no inheritance in the kingdom of God.” Ellicott explains “rich toward God” by the parallel call to be “rich in good works” (1 Timothy 6:18). Robertson: “the only wealth that matters and that lasts” (cf. Luke 16:9; Matthew 6:19).

The Reformed Calvin weighs the contrast: to be “rich toward God” is the opposite of laying up “for himself” — a wealth laid up with God, in his sight and for his glory, which death cannot touch.

So the witnesses gather the lesson of the rich fool into a single antithesis. The man’s ruin lay in the two words “for himself”: he gathered, but only for his own use; he was rich, but only toward the world. Over against this the Lord sets the true riches — to be “rich toward God,” laying up treasure in heaven by faith and good works and mercy to the poor. The witnesses press the application of the whole parable: not the having of goods but the hoarding of them for self, with no thought of God or of others, is the folly that death exposes. The wise man uses his abundance as a steward, and so is found rich where riches endure — not in barns that pass to another, but toward the God before whom the soul must give account.

So the witnesses gather the lesson of the rich fool into a single antithesis. The man’s ruin lay in the two words “for himself”: he gathered, but only for his own use; he was rich, but only toward the world. Over against this the Lord sets the true riches — to be “rich toward God,” which Ellicott explains as “rich in good works” (1 Timothy 6:18), and Robertson as “the only wealth that matters and that lasts.” The witnesses press the application of the whole parable: not the having of goods but the hoarding of them for self, with no thought of God or others, is the folly that death exposes. The wise man uses his abundance as a steward, and so is found rich where riches endure — toward the God before whom the soul must give account. The witnesses press the searching application to every heart: a man may be counted rich and wise by the world, his barns full and his future seemingly secure, and yet be a pauper before God, destitute of faith, of good works, of treasure in heaven; and the cure is to seek first the riches that endure — to be rich toward God, whose portion outlasts the grave and satisfies the soul.
22:
"And he said unto his disciples, Therefore I say unto you, Take no thought for your life, what ye shall eat; neither for the body, what ye shall put on." The witnesses note the turn from covetousness to anxiety and the meaning of "take no thought." Theophylact traces the progression: "He taught us above to beware of covetousness, and added the parable of the rich man; then He forbids us to be anxious even about necessary things, plucking out the very root of covetousness." Chrysostom guards against misreading: "the words, Take no thought, are not the same as do no work, but, Have not your minds fixed on earthly things; for it so happens that the man who is working takes no thought."

The witnesses note that Christ moves from the warning against hoarding superfluous wealth (the rich fool) to the deeper root — anxious care even about life's necessities, food and clothing.

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of the cure for anxiety. The lesson the expositors draw is the rooting out of covetousness at its source — anxious care — and the right understanding of "take no thought." The witnesses note Theophylact's observation of the progression: Christ has warned against amassing needless wealth; now he goes deeper, forbidding anxious care even about necessary things — food and raiment — for the root of covetousness is distrustful anxiety, and it must be plucked out at the source. The expositors are careful, with Chrysostom, to define what is and is not forbidden: "take no thought" does not mean "do no work" nor counsel idleness or improvidence; it forbids not honest labour and reasonable provision, but the anxious, distracting care that fixes the mind on earthly things and distrusts God. The diligent worker, the witnesses press, may yet be free of anxiety, casting his care on God; it is the corroding worry, not the working, that Christ condemns. The verse, addressed now to the disciples (where the foregoing parable was for the crowd), the expositors note, applies the lesson to those who would follow Christ: they are not to be consumed with care about life's necessities, as the rich fool was about his abundance, but to trust the providence of God. The verse thus turns from the folly of the covetous rich to the anxiety of the ordinary heart, beginning the great argument from God's care for the lesser creatures that the following verses unfold — the cure for the worry that besets even those who have renounced greed.

So the witnesses see the Lord pass from the gross sin of the rich fool to the subtler sin of the anxious disciple. Theophylact orders the whole: “He taught us above to beware of covetousness… Then… He forbids us to be anxious even about necessary things, plucking out the very root of covetousness.” Covetousness and anxiety spring from the same root — the belief that life depends on the abundance of things. Ellicott marks the shift of audience: the crowd was warned of hoarding, but “what follows is addressed to those… called to… a higher life.” The witnesses thus open the tender teaching on the ravens and the lilies, addressed not to the greedy but to the fearful, who fret over food and clothing as though God did not feed and clothe his own.
23:
"The life is more than meat, and the body is more than raiment." The witnesses note the argument from the greater to the lesser. The Reformed Gill draws it out: what Matthew puts as a question, Luke "strongly affirms"; the reasoning is "the life is more excellent and valuable in its own nature, being that for the support of which meat is provided; and seeing God is the author and giver of life, it need not be doubted but he will give meat for the maintenance of it, so long as is his pleasure it should subsist."

The witnesses note the force of the argument: God, who gave the greater gift — life itself, and the body — will surely supply the lesser things needed to sustain them, food and clothing.

So the witnesses make the verse the first argument against anxiety. The lesson the expositors draw is that the God who gave life and the body will not fail to provide their sustenance. The reasoning, the witnesses note, moves from the greater to the lesser: "the life is more than meat, and the body is more than raiment" — life and the body are the greater gifts, food and clothing the lesser, given only to sustain them. The expositors press the conclusion Gill draws: since God is the author of life, the very thing for whose support food exists, it would be strange to doubt that he who gave life would withhold the food to maintain it, or that he who made the body would deny it covering. To trust God for the greater and distrust him for the less, the witnesses observe, is unreasonable; the gift of life itself is the pledge of all the lesser gifts needed to preserve it. The expositors draw the comfort: the anxious soul, fretting over daily bread and raiment, may reason from God's greater bounty — he gave you life, he will give you what life requires, so long as he wills you to live. The verse thus lays the foundation of the whole argument that follows: the providence of God, proved by the greater gift, is the ground of confidence for the lesser; and the disciple, assured that the Giver of life will sustain it, may put away anxious care for food and clothing, trusting the Father who has already given so much more.

So the witnesses draw out the logic of trust. The same God who gave us life will not begrudge the bread that keeps it; the same God who fashioned the body will not leave it unclothed. Gill notes Luke states as fact what Matthew puts as a question — the life “more excellent and valuable in its own nature” than its food. Anxiety, the witnesses observe, reverses the right order, caring more for the means than the end, and doubting the Giver of the greater for the lesser. The verse thus grounds the call to freedom from care not in a careless optimism but in a sober inference: he who has given the body and the life may be trusted, beyond all anxious thought, to supply their humbler needs — and the disciple who weighs the gift already received will not torment himself over the gift still to come.
24:
"Consider the ravens: for they neither sow nor reap; which neither have storehouse nor barn; and God feedeth them: how much more are ye better than the fowls?" The witnesses note the argument from the birds and Luke's distinctive choice. Cyril draws the parallel with the earlier sparrows: "as before in raising us to boldness He assured us by the birds, You are of more value than many sparrows, so now from birds He conveys a firm trust." Bede explains "better": "you are more precious, because a rational animal like man is of a higher order than irrational things." He notes Christ might have cited men who cared least for earthly things — Elijah, Moses, John — "but He made mention of the birds, following the Old Testament, which sends us to the bee and the ant." Ambrose presses: "to the birds who have no labour of tilling, no produce of crops, Divine Providence grants unfailing sustenance... the cause of our poverty seems to be covetousness."

The witnesses note Luke's "ravens" (where Matthew has "birds") — the raven being unclean, improvident, even abandoning its young, yet fed by God.

So the witnesses make the verse the argument from God's feeding of the birds. The lesson the expositors draw is that the God who feeds the improvident ravens will surely feed his children. The witnesses note the force of the example: the ravens "neither sow nor reap," have neither storehouse nor barn, make no provision and lay up nothing — yet "God feedeth them." The expositors press the lesser-to-greater reasoning: if God's providence sustains creatures so improvident and lowly, how much more will he feed his rational children, who are of far higher worth — "better than the fowls"? Bede's note, the witnesses observe, marks the dignity of man, a rational creature of a higher order than the birds, and so the surer object of God's care. The choice of the raven, the witnesses note, sharpens the point: Luke names the very bird that Scripture marks as unclean and that was thought to neglect its young, yet even it is fed by God — a striking proof that divine providence reaches to the least deserving. Ambrose's reflection, the expositors press, turns it to rebuke: the birds, claiming nothing as their own, are freely sustained, while men's poverty and anxiety spring largely from covetousness, the grasping that God's free providence should shame. The verse thus presses the cure for anxiety: the disciple, beholding the ravens fed without barn or harvest, may trust that the Father who feeds them will not forget him, who is worth far more — and so may labour without the corroding care that distrust breeds.

So the witnesses find in the raven a daily sermon against worry. The bird that does no farming and keeps no barn — the very opposite of the rich fool with his hoarded harvest — is sustained by the open hand of God. Ambrose: to the birds, with “no labour of tilling… Divine Providence grants an unfailing sustenance”; Theophylact and Eusebius marvel that even the raven, which neglects its own young and scavenges with difficulty, lacks no food. The argument runs from the lesser to the greater: are not the disciples “much more” than the fowls? The witnesses thus set the trustfulness of nature against the faithlessness of anxious men, bidding the believer read in every raven a proof that the Father who feeds the careless birds will surely feed his children.
25:
"And which of you with taking thought can add to his stature one cubit?" The witnesses note the argument from the futility of anxiety, and a question of meaning. The grammatical-historical Robertson and Wesley note the phrase: Wesley observes "to add one cubit to a thing (the phrase in the original) was a kind of proverbial expression for making the least addition to it." Robertson notes "stature" (hēlikian) as in Matthew. Some take it of bodily height — a cubit being a large addition to one's stature — others, anticipating Ellicott's note on the next verse, of the measure of one's life or days.

The witnesses note the force: anxious care cannot accomplish even this small thing — to add a cubit to one's stature, or a moment to one's life — and so is proved useless.

So the witnesses make the verse the argument from anxiety's impotence. The lesson the expositors draw is the futility of anxious care, which cannot effect even the least addition to ourselves. The witnesses note the pointed question: "which of you with taking thought can add to his stature one cubit?" — whether the meaning be a cubit added to one's bodily height (a thing manifestly beyond all anxious striving) or, as some read it, a span added to the length of one's life. Either way, the expositors press, the argument is the same: worry is powerless to achieve its end. The anxious man frets as though his care could secure or lengthen his life, could add to himself what he lacks; but he cannot by all his anxiety add a single cubit to his frame or a single hour to his days — these lie wholly in the hand of God. Wesley's note, the witnesses observe, marks the phrase as proverbial for the smallest addition: if anxiety cannot do even this least thing, it is shown to be utterly vain. The expositors draw the lesson: since worry accomplishes nothing — cannot grow the body, cannot prolong life, cannot control the future — it is folly to indulge it; the wise course is to commit these things to God, who alone disposes them. The verse thus presses the cure for anxiety from another side: not only does God's providence supply our needs, but our own anxious care is powerless to add anything; and so the disciple, unable to better himself by worry, is freed to cast his care on the God in whose hand his life and measure rest.

So the witnesses press the unreasonableness of care. For all his anxious thought, a man cannot add a single cubit to his height nor a single hour to his life; the very thing he frets to secure lies wholly beyond his control and wholly in God’s hand. Wesley reads “to add one cubit” as a proverb for “the least addition”; Gill takes it of adding “one moment to his days.” Anxiety, the witnesses observe, not only distrusts God but accomplishes nothing. The rich fool could not, with all his barns, add a night to his life; the anxious disciple cannot, with all his fretting, add an inch to his frame. The cure is to see both the futility and the faithlessness of worry, and to leave with God the life and growth that are his alone to give.
26:
"If ye then be not able to do that thing which is least, why take ye thought for the rest?" The witnesses note this verse, peculiar to Luke, and its sharpened argument. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes: "the words are peculiar to St. Luke's report. If no amount of anxious care can add one cubit to our stature or the measure of our days, how much less can we control all the myriad contingencies upon which the happiness of the future may depend!" Robertson notes the verse is "not in Matthew," with "least" an elative superlative, "very small."

The witnesses note the reasoning from the least to the greater: if anxiety cannot do the smallest thing — add a cubit — it surely cannot manage the larger concerns of life, which lie still further beyond our control.

So the witnesses make the verse the clinching of the argument from anxiety's impotence. The lesson the expositors draw is that since worry cannot accomplish the least thing, it is doubly vain to be anxious about the rest. The witnesses note the force of the lesser-to-greater reasoning, here turned the other way: Christ has shown that anxious care cannot add even a cubit to one's stature, the smallest of things; and from this he argues, "If ye then be not able to do that which is least, why take ye thought for the rest?" The expositors press Ellicott's expansion: if no amount of worry can effect the least addition to ourselves, how much less can it control the countless larger contingencies on which our future depends — the events, circumstances, and uncertainties that lie wholly beyond our reach. The disciple, the witnesses observe, often frets most over the great and distant things — his future provision, his security, the shape of his life to come — yet these are precisely the things most beyond his power to determine. If he cannot manage the least, the expositors note, his anxiety over the greatest is the more clearly useless. Robertson's observation that the verse is Luke's alone, the witnesses note, marks it as the evangelist's drawing-out of the argument's logic. The verse thus completes the lesson on anxiety's futility: worry is powerless over both the least and the greatest, and so is shown to be wholly vain; the only reasonable course is to leave all — the small and the large, the present and the future — to the providence of God, who alone orders them, and to be free of the care that can change nothing.

So the witnesses press home the logic of trust. To add a cubit is the smallest of things, and even that lies beyond our power; how senseless, then, to be anxious about “the rest” — the larger matters of life — which are equally in God’s hand and beyond ours. Ellicott draws the inference: if no care can add a cubit, “how much less can we control all the myriad contingencies upon which the happiness of the future may depend!” The witnesses read here the Lord’s gentle reasoning with his anxious people: worry is doubly absurd, unable to achieve even the least and powerless over the great. The disciple is left with two options — to fret uselessly over what he cannot control, or to commit it to the God who controls all, and who feeds the ravens and clothes the lilies without their care.
27:
"Consider the lilies how they grow: they toil not, they spin not; and yet I say unto you, that Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these." The witnesses note the argument from the lilies and its careful balance. Chrysostom guards the meaning as he did with the birds: "as above when He said the birds sow not, He did not reprove sowing, but all superfluous trouble; so when He says, They toil not neither do they spin, He does not put an end to work, but to all anxiety about it." He notes the choice of the lily over grander things: "He does not employ the swan or peacock, but the lilies, for he wishes to give force to the argument both from the meanness of the things which have obtained such honour, and from the excellence of the honour conferred."

The witnesses note the contrast: the lilies, without toil or spinning, are clothed in a beauty surpassing Solomon's royal splendour — a glory given freely by God their Maker.

So the witnesses make the verse the argument from God's clothing of the flowers. The lesson the expositors draw is that the God who arrays the lilies in beauty will surely clothe his children. The witnesses note the force of the example: the lilies "toil not, they spin not" — they make no effort, no provision for their adorning — "and yet" they are clothed in a splendour beyond Solomon's, the richest and most glorious of kings, in all his royal array. The expositors press Chrysostom's careful balance: as with the birds, Christ does not condemn labour or spinning, but the anxious care about clothing; the lilies' example rebukes not industry but worry. And the choice of the lily, the witnesses observe (with Chrysostom), is deliberate: Christ takes not the swan or peacock, creatures of acknowledged beauty, but the common flower of the field, that the argument might gain force from both sides — from the meanness of the thing so gloriously adorned, and from the excellence of the adorning bestowed on so lowly a creature. If God so clothes the humble flower, the expositors draw, how much more will he clothe his children, who are of far greater worth? The witnesses note the rebuke to anxious care: the flowers, doing nothing for their own array, are made more glorious than Solomon by the free hand of their Maker; and the disciple, fretting over his raiment, is bidden to "consider" them and trust the same Maker. The verse thus presses the cure for anxiety from the clothing of the lilies, that the soul anxious for raiment might rest in the God who adorns the field.

So the witnesses set the glory of the lilies against the fret of anxious men. The wildflower, that does nothing for its own adorning, is robed by God more gloriously than Solomon; the Father who lavishes such art on a flower of a day will not leave his children unclothed. Chrysostom guards the lesson — “they toil not… neither” does this “put an end to work, but to superfluous trouble”; Eusebius marvels at God’s dyes “far superior to gold and purple”; Cyril bids the disciples “cast away all human cares.” The witnesses thus find in the lily, as in the raven, a daily preacher against worry: the God who paints the grass of the field with a splendour beyond a king’s wardrobe may be trusted, without distracting care, to clothe and feed those he has made his own.
28:
"If then God so clothe the grass, which is to day in the field, and to morrow is cast into the oven; how much more will he clothe you, O ye of little faith?" The witnesses note the lesser-to-greater argument and the gentle rebuke. The Reformed Gill notes the lilies "are no other than grass — weak, frail, fading, short-lived flowers, which have all their gaiety and beauty from the great Creator." Chrysostom (on v.27) had drawn out the force of "so clothe": Christ "says not simply, God clothe, but, God so clothe, which has much meaning"; and he reproves "not for unbelief, but for littleness of faith — O ye of little faith — that He may the more rouse us up to believe."

The witnesses note the contrast pressed to its conclusion: the grass, clothed in glory today and burned tomorrow, is so adorned by God; how much more will he clothe his enduring children, whose only fault is "little faith."

So the witnesses make the verse the conclusion of the argument from the lilies. The lesson the expositors draw is the certainty of God's provision and the gentle naming of anxiety as little faith. The witnesses note the force of the lesser-to-greater reasoning: the "grass" — for the lilies, Gill notes, are but flowers of the field, frail and short-lived, here today and cast into the oven tomorrow as fuel — is yet arrayed by God in a beauty surpassing Solomon's. If God so clothes a thing so mean and perishing, the expositors press, "how much more will he clothe you," his children, who are of enduring worth and dear to him?
