Chrysostom's observation, the witnesses observe, marks the emphasis: Christ says not merely "God clothes" but "God so clothes" — with such surpassing glory — and adds "how much more you," expressing the high value he sets on his people above the flowers. And the rebuke, the expositors note, is tender: Christ names the anxious not "unbelievers" but "ye of little faith" — diagnosing the root of all such worry as a deficiency of faith, yet naming it gently, that he might rouse them to believe rather than crush them with reproof. The witnesses draw the lesson: anxiety about food and raiment is, at bottom, a failure to trust the providence so abundantly displayed in the birds and the flowers; and the cure is faith — the faith that, beholding God's care for the grass, rests assured of his care for his children. The verse thus seals the argument: the God who clothes the perishing grass will surely clothe his people, and the only thing lacking is faith.

So the witnesses hear in the verse both comfort and reproof. The comfort is in the “how much more”: the God who clothes the fading grass — here today, tomorrow burned — will surely clothe the children he has redeemed. The reproof is in “O ye of little faith”: anxiety is, at bottom, a smallness of faith, a failure to trust the Father whose care for the least of his works is everywhere displayed. Gill marks that the lilies are “but grass, weak, frail, fading,” yet have all their beauty from the Creator. The witnesses note the tenderness even of the rebuke — not “no faith” but “little” — and the cure he presses: to look at the grass, to reason from the lesser to the greater, and so to let the sight of God’s lavish providence rebuke and heal the anxious heart.
29:
"And seek not ye what ye shall eat, or what ye shall drink, neither be ye of doubtful mind." The witnesses note the renewed exhortation and the distinctive phrase "doubtful mind." The Reformed Gill clarifies the prohibition: "that is, in an anxious and distressing manner, with a tormenting and vexatious care; otherwise food is to be both asked of God every day, and to be sought after in the use of proper means." The grammatical-historical Clarke and Robertson expound "be not of doubtful mind" (meteōrizesthe): Clarke, "in anxious suspense... to have the mind agitated with useless thoughts and vain imaginations concerning food, raiment, and riches, accompanied with perpetual uncertainty"; Robertson, from meteōros, "in midair, high (our meteor)," hence "to be anxious, in suspense." Robertson notes "stop seeking" (present imperative).

The witnesses note the vivid image: the anxious mind is "lifted up" and tossed in suspense, like a thing suspended in the air, unsettled and restless about earthly needs.

So the witnesses make the verse a fresh call to abandon anxious seeking. The lesson the expositors draw is the renunciation of the restless, suspended care that worry breeds. The witnesses note that Christ, having argued from the birds and the lilies, now presses the practical exhortation: "seek not ye what ye shall eat, or what ye shall drink" — and they are careful, with Gill, to define the seeking forbidden: not the lawful asking of daily bread from God, nor the diligent use of proper means to provide it, but the "anxious and distressing" seeking, the "tormenting and vexatious care" that distrusts God and consumes the soul. The added phrase, peculiar in its vividness, the expositors press, deepens the picture: "neither be ye of doubtful mind" — the Greek, Robertson and Clarke note, drawn from a word for being suspended in midair, depicts the anxious mind as "lifted up" and tossed, hanging in restless suspense, agitated by uncertainty about food, raiment, and riches. This wavering, unsettled state, the witnesses observe, is the very opposite of the steady trust Christ commends; the believer is not to hang fluttering between hope and fear over earthly provision, but to rest his mind on the providence of God. The expositors draw the lesson: anxiety is not only futile and faithless but disquieting, holding the soul in perpetual suspense; and Christ bids his disciples come down from that restless height, ceasing both the anxious seeking and the doubtful mind, to a settled confidence in their Father's care. The verse thus calls for the inward peace that faith in providence brings, the end of the tossing suspense of worry.

So the witnesses press the command home with its vivid added word. It is not seeking food by lawful labour that is forbidden, but the anxious, distracted seeking — the mind “of doubtful mind” (meteorizesthe), tossed like a meteor between hope and dread, “suspended… in an uneasy hesitation” (Wesley, Clarke). The emphatic “ye” sets the disciples apart: let the heathen so live, but not the children of the Father who feeds the ravens. Calvin marks the restless mind that “would willingly embrace” whole centuries in its arrangements. The witnesses thus distinguish diligence from anxiety: the believer works and asks his daily bread, but does not let his mind be agitated by the fear of want — for to be of “doubtful mind” is to live as though he had no Father in heaven.
30:
"For all these things do the nations of the world seek after: and your Father knoweth that ye have need of these things." The witnesses note the two motives against anxiety: it is heathenish, and the Father knows our needs. The Reformed Gill explains "the nations of the world": "the Gentiles, frequently in Jewish writings so called in distinction from the Jews... this is an argument to dissuade from anxious concern for food and raiment, because it is heathenish, and unbecoming the disciples of Christ; it need not be wondered at in those who know not God, nor his providence... but must be very unsuitable to such who know that godliness has the promise of this life and of that to come." Lightfoot confirms "the nations of the world" as the common Jewish phrase for the Gentiles. Clarke presses "seek after" (epizētei): "to seek one thing after another, continually and eagerly coveting — the employment of the nations of this world, utterly regardless of God and eternity."

The witnesses note the two grounds: such anxious seeking marks the heathen, who know not God; and the disciple's Father already knows his needs.

So the witnesses make the verse the double argument that crowns the cure for anxiety. The lesson the expositors draw is that anxious care is unbecoming the children of God, both because it is heathenish and because the Father already knows their needs. First, the witnesses note, Christ marks such seeking as the way of "the nations of the world" — the Gentiles, who know not God nor his providence, and so, as Clarke presses, eagerly covet one thing after another, living only for this life, regardless of God and eternity. For the heathen, the expositors observe, such anxiety is natural; but for the disciples of Christ, who know the Father and his promises, it is utterly unsuitable — to fret over food and raiment as the godless do is to deny the very faith they profess, that godliness has the promise of this life and the next. Second, and more tenderly, the witnesses note the assurance: "your Father knoweth that ye have need of these things." The expositors press the comfort: the disciple's God is his Father, who knows his needs before he asks, and who, knowing, will surely supply — so that anxious seeking is needless as well as faithless, the child fretting for what the Father already plans to give. The verse thus seals the whole argument with the sweetest of motives: the believer has a Father in heaven who knows and cares for his every need, and to be anxious is both to act like the heathen who have no such Father and to distrust the Father he has. The cure for anxiety, the witnesses press, is to rest in this fatherly knowledge and love, seeking first the kingdom and trusting the rest to God.

So the witnesses gather the cure for worry into its deepest ground: the Fatherhood of God. Gill and Lightfoot note “the nations of the world” is a Jewish phrase for the Gentiles, so that anxious care is “heathenish… unbecoming the disciples of Christ”; Clarke, that “it is the essence of heathenism to live only for this life.” Against this is set the decisive second motive — “your Father knoweth that ye have need.” The Gentile, having no Father, must seek these things for himself with eager, covetous care; but the disciple has a Father who knows his needs before he asks. The witnesses thus press the contrast that closes the section: the world’s feverish pursuit springs from fatherlessness, but the child of God may lay his care aside, knowing the One who feeds the ravens and clothes the lilies is his Father.
31:
"But rather seek ye the kingdom of God; and all these things shall be added unto you." The witnesses note the great command to seek God's kingdom first, the antidote to anxious care. Gill expounds the "kingdom": "either the grace of God, which is not meat and drink, but righteousness, peace and joy in the Holy Ghost, or the heavenly glory; unless the Gospel, and the dispensation of it, which is the Messiah's kingdom... should rather be intended." The kingdom sought is the rule of God's grace in the heart and the glory to come.

The grammatical voices note Luke's shorter form. Robertson: "Luke does not have first nor his righteousness nor all" (against Matthew 6:33). Barnes cross-references the parallel and the promise of provision (Psalm 34:10; Romans 8:31-32). The promise "all these things shall be added" assures the seeker of the kingdom that his temporal needs (food, raiment) will be supplied — the very anxieties Christ had been removing (vv.22-30).

The command the witnesses read as the right ordering of desire. Instead of the anxious seeking of the nations after food and clothing (v.30), the disciple is to seek the kingdom; and the Father, who knows his needs, will add the rest. The seeking of the kingdom both displaces worldly anxiety and secures the provision the anxious vainly pursue.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: in place of anxious care for food and raiment, Christ commands the disciple to seek the kingdom of God — the rule of his grace and the glory to come (Gill) — with the promise that all temporal needs will be added. Luke's shorter form (omitting Matthew's "first," "his righteousness," "all," Robertson) presses the single point: seek the kingdom, and trust the Father for the rest. The verse displays the right ordering of the believer's desire: the kingdom first, the temporal supplied as it follows. To seek the kingdom is both to displace the anxious seeking of the nations and to secure, by the Father's promise, the very provision that anxiety vainly chases. The disciple, freed from care, sets his heart on God's kingdom, and finds his needs met by the Father who knows them. The verse is the positive counterpart to the warning against anxiety: not bare freedom from care, but the active seeking of the kingdom, is the believer's calling — and the sure way to have all that is needful added. Seek first the kingdom, and the rest follows.
32:
"Fear not, little flock; for it is your Father's good pleasure to give you the kingdom." The witnesses note the tender encouragement to the "little flock." The fathers explain the smallness. Theophylact: "the Saints seem little because of their voluntary poverty, or because they are outnumbered by the multitude of Angels... either from comparison with the greater number of the reprobate, or rather because of their devout humility." Cyril: "although His Flock is little both in nature and number and renown, yet the goodness of the Father has granted even to this little flock... the kingdom of heaven."

The grammatical voices note the word. Clarke reads "very little flock, a double diminutive, little little flock," though Robertson corrects: "Poimnion (flock) is a contraction... So it is not a diminutive and mikron is not superfluous, though it is pathetic." Ellicott: "the little flock (the Greek has the article) to whom the Father was pleased to give the kingdom which is righteousness and peace and joy."

The Reformed Calvin stresses the free grace in "good pleasure": "the verb eudokein conveys the idea that eternal life flows to us from the fountain of undeserved mercy... When Christ plainly declares that God gives the kingdom, and for no other reason but because it so pleased him, it is perfectly manifest that it is not obtained by any merits of works." The kingdom is the Father's free gift to his little flock.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ tenderly bids his "little flock" — little in number, worldly esteem, and humility, outnumbered by the reprobate and the angels (Theophylact, Cyril) — not to fear, for it is the Father's good pleasure to give them the kingdom. Calvin draws the free grace: the kingdom flows from undeserved mercy, given because it pleased the Father, not earned by works. The verse displays the comfort of the gospel against fear: the disciples, few and despised, need not fear want or weakness, for the Father who freely gives them the kingdom will surely supply their lesser needs (Wesley: "how much more food and raiment?"). The smallness of the flock is no cause for fear but the occasion of greater grace, for the Father delights to give the kingdom to the little, humble company of the elect. The little flock is rich, not poor, for the kingdom is theirs by the free good pleasure of the Father — and they who possess the kingdom need fear nothing.
33:
"Sell that ye have, and give alms; provide yourselves bags which wax not old, a treasure in the heavens that faileth not, where no thief approacheth, neither moth corrupteth." The witnesses note the command to liberality and treasure in heaven, and debate its scope. The fathers commend almsgiving. Chrysostom: "there is no sin which almsgiving does not avail to blot out... without alms it is impossible to see the kingdom." Basil: the command teaches "not to cast away as evil what we possess, but to distribute."

On the scope, the witnesses qualify. Bede: "this must not be taken as a command that no money be kept by the saints... since we read that our Lord Himself had a bag... but that God should not be obeyed for the sake of such things." Wesley: "This is a direction, not given to all the multitude, much less a standing rule for all Christians... but to his other disciples, especially the seventy, that they might be free from all worldly entanglements." Robertson defends Luke against the charge of Ebionism: "Jesus does not condemn property as inherently sinful," and notes Plummer's observation that the literal attempt (Acts 2:44-45) "had disastrous effects on the church of Jerusalem, which speedily became a church of paupers." Ellicott: "the principle implied, that it is wise to sit loose to earthly possessions... has not lost its force."

The "treasure in heaven" the witnesses read as the abiding reward of liberality. The bags that wax not old, the treasure that fails not, beyond thief and moth, figure the imperishable reward of giving for Christ's sake. Clarke: "he who hath pity on the poor lendeth unto the Lord."

The witnesses agree on the substance — Christ commands liberality and the laying up of treasure in heaven, which no thief or moth can reach — while differing on the scope: a literal, binding rule (fulfilled in Acts 2:45), or a special direction to the disciples/Seventy and a principle for all to sit loose to possessions (Wesley, Bede, Ellicott, Robertson). The fathers exalt almsgiving as the blotter of sin and the path to the kingdom (Chrysostom). The verse displays the right use of earthly goods: to be held loosely, distributed in charity, and so exchanged for imperishable treasure in heaven. Whether taken as a literal command of total renunciation or as the principle of generous detachment, the teaching is one: the believer, freed from anxious care (v.31) and assured of the kingdom (v.32), holds his possessions as a steward, gives liberally to the poor, and lays up treasure where it cannot perish. The heart follows the treasure (v.34); and the treasure laid up in heaven secures the heart there.
34:
"For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also." The brief verse gives the ground of the command to lay up heavenly treasure. Gill: "the heart is always set upon the treasure, and as is a man's treasure, such is his heart... if his treasure is heavenly, his heart and conversation will be in heaven." The heart inevitably follows the treasure; where a man's wealth is, there his affections dwell.

The fathers (under v.32) read it of the soul's direction. Eusebius: "every man naturally dwells upon that which is the object of his desire... If any one has his whole mind and affections, which he calls the heart, set on things of this present life, he lives in earthly things. But if he has given his mind to heavenly things, there will his mind be." Theophylact: even if neither moth nor thief touch the buried treasure, "to have the heart fixed in a buried treasure, and to sink to the earth a divine work, that is, the soul, how great a punishment it deserves."

The grammatical voices note the universal principle. Clarke cites the parallel from Plautus: "I am here; but my heart is at home, i.e. with my money." Where the treasure is, the heart follows — a truth as evident in the covetous man's anxious thoughts as in the believer's heavenward affections.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the heart inevitably follows the treasure — where a man's wealth and desire are, there his affections dwell (Gill, Eusebius). This is the ground of the command to lay up heavenly treasure (v.33): to set the treasure in heaven is to draw the heart heavenward; to set it on earth is to sink the soul, a divine work, into the dust (Theophylact). The verse displays the inseparable link between treasure and heart: the affections cannot but follow what we most value. The covetous man's heart is anxiously with his money; the believer's heart, with his treasure in heaven. Therefore the disciple is to lay up treasure above, that his heart may dwell there — for the heart will surely be where the treasure is. The verse searches every soul: where is your treasure? For there, whether you will or no, your heart already is. To love heavenly treasure is to live a heavenly life; and to fix the treasure on earth is to bury the soul. The treasure laid up determines the home of the heart.
35:
"Let your loins be girded about, and your lights burning." The witnesses note the call to readiness, opening the parable of the waiting servants. The fathers read the imagery richly. Theophylact: "Let your loins be girded, that is, always ready to do the work of your Lord; and your lamps burning, that is, do not lead a life in darkness, but have with you the light of reason." Gregory: "we gird our loins when by continence we control the lusts of the flesh... we hold burning lamps in our hands when by good works we show forth bright examples to our neighbours." The girded loins figure active, chaste readiness; the burning lamps, good works and discernment.

The grammatical voices explain the Eastern custom. Barnes: "They wore a long flowing robe... When they laboured, it was necessary to gird or tie this up... to gird up the loins means to be ready, to be active, to be diligent." Ellicott: "to gird up the loins was the received symbol of readiness for active service"; the lights are "the lamps which the watchful hold in their hands." Robertson notes "the same point of the Parable of the Ten Virgins is found here in condensed form."

The call the witnesses read as the summons to constant readiness for the Lord's return. The disciple, having sought the kingdom and laid up heavenly treasure, must live in active, watchful readiness — girded for service, his lamp burning — awaiting the Lord who will come at an unexpected hour (v.40).

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the parable of the waiting servants. The witnesses agree: Christ summons his disciples to constant readiness — loins girded for active service, lamps burning in their hands. The fathers read the imagery: the girded loins figure chaste, active diligence (continence controlling the flesh, Gregory); the burning lamps, good works and discernment shining before others (Theophylact, Gregory). The Eastern custom of tucking up the long robe for labour underlies the figure of readiness (Barnes, Ellicott). The verse, the condensed germ of the Ten Virgins (Robertson), displays the posture of the waiting disciple: not idle repose but active, watchful readiness for the Lord's return. Having sought the kingdom (v.31) and laid up heavenly treasure (v.33), the believer is to live girded and burning — ready at every moment to meet his returning Lord. The girded loins and burning lamp are the marks of the faithful servant who waits in readiness, neither slothful nor in darkness, but active in service and bright in good works, awaiting the Master's coming.
36:
"And ye yourselves like unto men that wait for their lord, when he will return from the wedding; that when he cometh and knocketh, they may open unto him immediately." The witnesses note the figure of servants awaiting their lord's return. The fathers read the wedding. Gregory: "our Lord went to the wedding when ascending up into heaven; as the Bridegroom He joined to Himself the heavenly multitude of angels." Theophylact: "Daily also in the heavens He betroths the souls of the Saints... He returns from the heavenly marriage perhaps to all at the end of the world, perhaps also every hour standing suddenly present at the death of each individual."

The grammatical voices note the imagery. Robertson: the verb "analyo... the figure is breaking up a camp or loosening the mooring of a ship, to depart." The servants are to be so ready that "when he cometh and knocketh, they may open immediately."

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson of vigilant duty: "though the Son of God has departed to the blessed rest of heaven, and is absent from us, yet as he has assigned to every one his duty, it would be improper for us to give way to indolent repose. Besides, as he has promised that he will return to us, we ought to hold ourselves prepared, at every moment, to receive him." The servants' readiness to open at once figures the believer's constant preparedness for the Lord's return, whether at death or at the last day.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the disciples are to be like servants awaiting their lord's return from the wedding, ready to open the moment he knocks. The fathers read the wedding as Christ's ascension and heavenly union with the angels and the souls of the saints; his return "from the wedding" as his coming at the end of the world, or to each at death (Gregory, Theophylact). Calvin draws the lesson: though Christ is absent in heaven, the believer must not sink into indolent repose but hold himself ready at every moment to receive his returning Lord. The verse displays the posture of vigilant readiness: the servant who waits, listening for the knock, ready to open immediately, figures the disciple who lives in constant preparedness for Christ's return. Whether the Lord comes at the last day or at the hour of death, the faithful are to be found waiting and ready — not asleep, but watching, that they may open to him at once and be received with joy (v.37). The returning Bridegroom is to find his servants awake and eager to welcome him.
37:
"Blessed are those servants, whom the lord when he cometh shall find watching: verily I say unto you, that he shall gird himself, and make them to sit down to meat, and will come forth and serve them." The witnesses note the astonishing reward of the watchful servants — the Lord himself serving them. Barnes marvels: "Shall take the place of the servant himself... This shows the exceeding kindness and condescension of our Lord. For us, poor and guilty sinners, he denied himself, took the form of a servant (Philippians 2:7), and ministered to our wants." Ellicott connects it to the foot-washing: "The words give a new significance to the act of our Lord in John 13:4," and to Revelation 3:20 ("I will sup with him").

The grammatical voices note the foreshadowing. Robertson: "Jesus did this (John 13:4), not out of gratitude, but to give the apostles an object lesson in humility." Lightfoot: "the master will pass by his dignity, and condescend to minister to his own servants." The reward is the Lord's own gracious service of his faithful, found watching at his coming.

The fathers read the future blessedness. Theophylact: "He will make them to sit down; for as a man sitting down causes his whole body to rest, so in the future coming the Saints will have complete rest." Dionysius: "the sitting down is the repose from many labours... an abundant pouring forth of all gifts, whereby they are filled with joy." Christ's serving them figures the heavenly feast and rest he will give his faithful servants.

The thin comment matches the rich verse. The witnesses agree: the watchful servants, found ready at the Lord's coming, receive an astonishing reward — the Lord himself girds himself, seats them at his table, and serves them. This is the exceeding condescension of Christ, who took the servant's form for us (Philippians 2:7, Barnes), foreshadowed in the foot-washing (John 13:4, Ellicott, Robertson) and promised in the heavenly feast (Revelation 3:20). The fathers read the "sitting down" as the saints' complete rest, and the serving as the abundant outpouring of gifts and joy in the kingdom (Theophylact, Dionysius). The verse displays the unimaginable grace of the gospel: the Master who could rightly be served by his servants instead serves them — giving them rest, feasting them at his table, ministering to them in the kingdom. The watchful servant's reward is not merely admission but the personal, gracious service of his Lord. Such is the condescension of Christ, who delights to honour and refresh his faithful, found watching at his coming, with the joys of his own table and the rest of his kingdom.
38:
"And if he shall come in the second watch, or come in the third watch, and find them so, blessed are those servants." The witnesses note the watches of the night and the blessedness of constant readiness. The grammatical voices explain the division. Ellicott: "Here we find the older Jewish division into three watches (Judges 7:19; 1 Samuel 11:11)," as distinct from the Roman fourfold division (Mark 13:35). Lightfoot: the second and third watches were "the very dead time of sleep" — when readiness is hardest and most tested.

The fathers (under v.35) read the watches as the ages of life. Gregory: "The first watch is the earliest time of our life, that is, childhood; the second youth and manhood; the third old age. He then who is unwilling to watch in the first, let him keep even the second... that he who has neglected conversion in childhood may at least in youth or old age recover himself." The Greek Exposition and Theophylact read the watches as the varying measures of life and the times of trial when the watchful are found ready.

The blessedness the witnesses read as the reward of perseverance. The servant found watching even in the dead of night — even in the late watches, even in the hardest seasons of trial or age — is blessed. The point is constant readiness: whenever the Lord comes, at whatever watch, the faithful are to be found awake.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the servant found watching even in the second or third watch — the dead of night, when sleep is heaviest (Lightfoot), the Jewish threefold division (Ellicott) — is blessed. The fathers read the watches as the ages of life (childhood, youth, old age, Gregory): he who neglects to watch in youth must at least watch in age, that he be found ready whenever the Lord comes. The verse displays the blessedness of constant, persevering readiness: the Lord may come at any watch, and the faithful are to be found awake at every one. Whether he comes early or late, in youth or age, in ease or trial, the watchful servant is blessed. The lesson is perseverance: not a readiness that flags as the night wears on, but a vigilance that endures to the last watch. The Lord who delays may come at the most unlikely hour; and blessed is the servant whom, at whatever watch, he finds watching. The whole night, the whole life, is to be spent in readiness for the Master's return.
39:
"And this know, that if the goodman of the house had known what hour the thief would come, he would have watched, and not have suffered his house to be broken through." The witnesses note the second illustration — the thief — applied to the Lord's unexpected coming. Robertson: "The change here almost makes a new parable to illustrate the other, the parable of the housebreaking to illustrate the parable of the waiting servants. The Master returning from a wedding is replaced by a thief whose study it is to come to the house he means to plunder at an unexpected time" (Bruce).

The grammatical voices note the point of unexpectedness. The householder, not knowing the hour of the thief, would have watched had he known. The figure stresses the suddenness and unexpectedness of the Lord's coming — "the day of the Lord so comes as a thief in the night" (1 Thessalonians 5:2; Theophylact, under v.35, and the cross-references).

The fathers (under v.35) note the limit of the figure. Theophylact: "the Lord's coming is compared to the thief as suddenly at hand," not in the sense of stealing, but of unexpectedness. Gregory: "unknown to the master the thief breaks into the house, because while the spirit sleeps instead of guarding itself, death comes unexpectedly." The figure warns of the suddenness of the Lord's coming and of death.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the illustration of the thief (a new figure to reinforce the waiting servants, Robertson) stresses the unexpectedness of the Lord's coming. As the householder, ignorant of the hour, would have watched had he known the thief was coming, so the disciple, knowing the Lord will come at an unknown hour, must watch always. The figure (the Lord coming "as a thief in the night," 1 Thessalonians 5:2) signifies not stealth but suddenness (Theophylact). The verse displays the reason for constant readiness: because the hour is unknown, vigilance must be unceasing. Had the householder known the hour, he would have watched then only; but since the Lord's coming (and death itself, Gregory) is sudden and unannounced, the disciple must watch at all times. The very uncertainty of the hour is the ground of perpetual readiness (v.40). The thief comes when least expected; and so the Son of man comes at an hour we think not — therefore the believer must be ready always, lest the house of his soul be broken through while he sleeps.
40:
"Be ye therefore ready also: for the Son of man cometh at an hour when ye think not." The witnesses note the application of the parable — constant readiness, because the hour is unknown. Gill: "Not habitually, but actually, in the exercise of grace, and the discharge of duty, with loins girt, and lights burning." He notes the double reference: "either of a readiness to meet the Lord in the way of his judgments, and particularly the destruction of Jerusalem... or of a preparation for death, and the last judgment."

The grammatical voices note the present imperative. Robertson: "Be ye (ginesthe). Present middle imperative, keep on becoming." The readiness is not a single act but a continual state — always becoming ready, always prepared. Cyril of Jerusalem connects it to the signs of the end and the warning against curiosity about the time: "it is not for you to know times or seasons... Watch, for in such an hour as ye expect not the Son of Man cometh."

The unknown hour the witnesses read as the very ground of the command. Precisely because the Son of man comes "at an hour when ye think not," the disciple must be ready always. The uncertainty of the time is not a license for delay but the reason for perpetual preparedness.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, the application of the parable. The witnesses agree: because the Son of man comes at an unexpected hour, the disciple must be ready always — actually ready, in the exercise of grace and duty, with loins girt and lamp burning (Gill). The present imperative "keep on becoming ready" (Robertson) marks a continual state, not a single act. The readiness is for the Lord's coming in judgment (the destruction of Jerusalem), at death, and at the last day. The verse crowns the parable of the waiting servants with its plain lesson: watch, because the hour is unknown. The uncertainty of the time is the ground of constant preparedness; for the Lord who delays may come at the least expected moment, and the unready will be caught as by a thief. The believer is therefore to live in perpetual readiness, neither presuming on delay nor curious about the time, but watchful at every hour — for the Son of man comes when we think not. To be always ready is the only safety; and the disciple who lives girded and burning need not fear the suddenness of his Lord's return.
41:
"Then Peter said unto him, Lord, speakest thou this parable unto us, or even to all?" The witnesses note Peter's question and Christ's indirect answer, introducing the parable of the steward. Bede weighs the question: "by these words, us and all, he must be supposed to mean none other than the Apostles, and those like to the Apostles, and all other faithful men... It remains to be supposed that knowing these two things [the sudden coming and the need to watch], he asked whether those sublime commands... belonged to the Apostles only, and those like them, or to all who were to be saved."

The puritan Henry draws the lesson: "Peter desires Christ to explain himself, and to direct the arrow of the foregoing parable to the mark he intended... To this Christ gives a direct answer (Mark 13:37): What I say unto you, I say unto all. Yet here he seems to show that the apostles were primarily concerned... We are all concerned to take to ourselves what Christ in his word designs for us." The grammatical voices note Christ's reply by a question (v.42): Robertson, "Jesus introduces this parable of the wise steward by a rhetorical question that answers itself. Peter is this wise steward, each of the Twelve is, anyone is who acts thus."

The fathers read the steward as the minister especially. Ambrose: "the form of the first command is a general one adapted to all, but the following example seems to be proposed to the stewards, that is, the priests." Theophylact: "in the things of God there is need of faithfulness and wisdom," for an unwise or unfaithful steward destroys "not only possessions, but souls."

The witnesses agree: Peter asks whether the parable of watchfulness was meant for the apostles or for all; and Christ answers indirectly by the parable of the steward (v.42), implying that while watchfulness is required of all, the stewardship is especially the duty of ministers. The reply, "What I say unto you, I say unto all" (Mark 13:37, Henry), applies the watchfulness to all believers, yet the steward-figure that follows is "primarily adapted to ministers, who are the stewards in Christ's house" (Henry, Ambrose, Theophylact). The verse displays both the personal application every believer should make of Christ's word ("speakest thou to us?") and the special responsibility of those set over God's household. The faithful steward must have both faithfulness and wisdom, for the unfaithful or unwise destroys souls (Theophylact). The verse opens the parable of the steward, which sets before ministers especially — and all believers in their measure — the duty of faithful, wise stewardship, and the reward or judgment that follows. Christ's word is for all, yet each must ask how it applies to himself.
42:
"And the Lord said, Who then is that faithful and wise steward, whom his lord shall make ruler over his household, to give them their portion of meat in due season?" The witnesses note the description of the faithful steward, especially the minister. Gill: "Such are stewards in Christ's family, they are entrusted with the stores and provisions of his house, and faithfulness and wisdom are requisite in them; the one, that they do not corrupt and adulterate the word of God... and the other, that they may rightly divide it, and wisely distribute it."

The puritan Henry expounds the steward's duty: "They are made rulers of God's household, under Christ... Their business is to give God's children and servants their portion of meat, that which is proper for them... convictions and comfort to those to whom they respectively belong. Suum cuique — to every one his own. This is rightly to divide the word of truth (2 Timothy 2:15)... To give it to them in due season... Herein they must approve themselves faithful and wise."

The grammatical voices note the terms. Robertson: "A steward is house manager... Each man is a steward in his own responsibilities... Their portion of food (to sitometrion), late word... to measure the food, the rations." The faithful and wise steward dispenses the household's food (the word of God) in due season, suited to each.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the faithful and wise steward — especially the minister — is set over God's household to give each member his portion of food in due season. Faithfulness keeps him from corrupting the word; wisdom enables him to divide it rightly, suiting each hearer's need (Gill, Henry). The verse displays the office and duty of the steward in Christ's house: to dispense the word of God (the household's food) faithfully and wisely, "to every one his own" (Henry, 2 Timothy 2:15), and "in due season," fitted to each soul's condition. The minister is a steward, not an owner — entrusted with the stores of Christ's house for the benefit of his fellow-servants. The two requisites are faithfulness (to the Master and the word) and wisdom (in distributing it aptly). The verse sets the standard of faithful ministry, and prepares for the blessing of the faithful steward (vv.43-44) and the judgment of the unfaithful (vv.45-46). The steward who feeds the household well, in due season, is the model of the faithful servant of Christ.
43:
"Blessed is that servant, whom his lord when he cometh shall find so doing." The brief verse pronounces the blessing of the faithful steward. Gill: "Who is faithful and wise, rules well, and discharges his trust with integrity, and disposes of the provisions of the house, in his hands, with great discretion and prudence." The blessing falls on the servant found at his duty when the lord returns.

The fathers (under v.42) note the emphasis on "so doing." Basil: "He says not, doing, as if by chance, but so doing. For not only conquest is honourable, but to contend lawfully, which is to perform each thing as we have been commanded." Isidore Peleus: the food given "in due season," for "a benefit not conferred at its proper time loses the name of a benefit." The blessing is on the steward found faithfully discharging his trust in the appointed manner.

Wesley sounds a solemn note: "God himself pronounces him wise, faithful, happy! Yet we see he might fall from all, and perish for ever" (anticipating v.45). The blessing is real, but conditional on continued faithfulness; the same servant, if he turns to abuse (v.45), forfeits all.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the faithful steward found "so doing" — discharging his trust with integrity and discretion (Gill), performing his duty in the appointed manner, not by chance but as commanded (Basil) — is pronounced blessed by his Lord. The emphasis on "so doing" marks the faithful, seasonable discharge of duty (Isidore). Wesley adds the solemn warning that this same blessed servant might yet fall (v.45). The verse displays the blessedness of the faithful minister and servant: to be found, at the Lord's coming, faithfully at his appointed work. The blessing is not for the servant who merely holds the office, but for the one found actively, faithfully discharging it. The steward who feeds the household well, in due season, and is found so doing when his Lord returns, is blessed — and shall be advanced to greater trust (v.44). Yet the blessing presses the warning: continue faithful, for the servant who turns to abuse forfeits all. To be found "so doing" is the mark of the blessed servant.
44:
"Of a truth I say unto you, that he will make him ruler over all that he hath." The brief verse promises the faithful steward's advancement. Gill: "this, with Christ, was a strong way of asserting the truth of any thing... you may assure yourselves of it, this will certainly be the happy case of such servant." The solemn "of a truth" guarantees the reward.

The fathers (under v.42) read the advancement as the joys of the kingdom. Bede: "He will place over all that He has, that is, over all the joys of the kingdom of heaven, not that they alone shall have power over them, but that they shall more abundantly than the other saints enjoy eternal possession of them." Cyril: the faithful steward "will obtain still greater things, and will be thought worthy of the rewards which are due to friends." The reward for faithful stewardship is greater trust and fuller joy in the kingdom.

The grammatical voices note the parallel. Robertson refers to Matthew 24:47 for the same promise. The faithful servant is advanced from steward over the household to ruler over all the master's goods — a figure of the believer's promotion to fuller enjoyment of the kingdom.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of this section. The witnesses agree: with the solemn "of a truth" (Christ's strong asseveration, Gill), the faithful steward is promised advancement — made ruler over all his lord's goods. The fathers read this as the fuller enjoyment of the kingdom's joys: the faithful steward, advanced beyond the household to all the master has, will more abundantly possess the joys of heaven and be reckoned among the Lord's friends (Bede, Cyril). The verse displays the reward of faithful stewardship: not merely continuance in office, but promotion to greater trust and fuller joy. The servant faithful over the household is set over all; the believer faithful in his stewardship is advanced to the abundant possession of the kingdom. The reward far exceeds the service: for a little faithfulness, the Lord grants rule over all he has. The verse crowns the blessing of the faithful steward (v.43) with the promise of advancement, encouraging every servant of Christ to faithfulness by the assurance of a great and certain reward. The faithful steward's promotion is the pledge of the joy that awaits all who serve Christ well.
45:
"But and if that servant say in his heart, My lord delayeth his coming; and shall begin to beat the menservants and maidens, and to eat and drink, and to be drunken." The witnesses note the evil servant — the unfaithful steward who presumes on his lord's delay. Gill distinguishes him: "not the same servant before spoken of as a wise and faithful steward... but another, who also, as he, is made by his Lord ruler over his household, and is in a like post, and in the same office, but is an evil servant." The unfaithful steward holds the same office but betrays it.

The fathers (under v.41) read the abuse. Bede: "In this servant is declared the condemnation of all evil rulers, who, forsaking the fear of the Lord, not only give themselves up to pleasures, but also provoke with injuries those who are put under them." The grammatical voices note the cause: the servant says "in his heart, My lord delayeth," and so falls to abuse and indulgence. Robertson: "Delayeth (chronizei)... spends time, lingers." The presumption of delay is the root of his fall.

The evil servant the witnesses read as a warning to ministers and all in trust. The one who, presuming on the Lord's delay, abuses his fellow-servants and gives himself to indulgence, is the picture of the unfaithful steward — holding the office but betraying the trust. The delay that should have prompted faithfulness instead emboldens him to wickedness.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the evil servant — holding the same office as the faithful steward but betraying it (Gill) — presumes on his lord's delay ("My lord delayeth his coming") and falls to abusing his fellow-servants and indulging in drunkenness. The fathers read this as the condemnation of all evil rulers who, losing the fear of God, give themselves to pleasure and oppress those under them (Bede). The root of his fall is the presumption of delay (Robertson): supposing the Lord far off, he casts off restraint. The verse displays the peril of the unfaithful steward, a warning especially to ministers: the same office that brings blessing to the faithful brings condemnation to the abuser. The delay of the Lord's coming, which should prompt the faithful to greater diligence (v.43), instead emboldens the wicked servant to tyranny and self-indulgence. Presuming on delay, he forgets the certain reckoning (v.46). The verse sets the dark counterpart to the faithful steward: not the absence of office, but the abuse of it, is his ruin. To presume on the Lord's delay is the beginning of the unfaithful servant's fall.
46:
"The lord of that servant will come in a day when he looketh not for him, and at an hour when he is not aware, and will cut him in sunder, and will appoint him his portion with the unbelievers." The witnesses note the sudden judgment of the unfaithful steward. The grammatical voices explain "cut in sunder." Robertson: "dichotomeo... to cut in two. Used literally here." The fathers (under v.41) read it variously. Basil: "the body is not divided... but its division is the eternal severing of the soul from the Spirit." Bede: "He will cut him in sunder, by separating him from the communion of the faithful."

On "with the unbelievers," the witnesses note the better sense. Clarke: "rather, the unfaithful... Persons who had the light and knowledge of God's word, but made an improper use of the privileges they received." Ellicott: "the unfaithful, the word affording sharp contrast with the faithful and wise steward." Robertson: "the unreliable, the untrustworthy. Here Matthew 24:51 has with the hypocrites, the same point."

The sudden coming the witnesses read as the certainty of reckoning. Gill: "not coming as was expected, he gives over looking, and concludes he will not come at all; in which he will find himself mistaken." The Lord comes at the unexpected hour and judges the abuser, appointing him a portion with the faithless. Wesley: "His everlasting portion, with the unfaithful — As faithful as he was once, God himself being the Judge!"

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the lord of the unfaithful servant comes suddenly, at an unexpected hour, and judges him — cutting him in sunder (literally, the eternal severing of soul from the Spirit, Basil; or separating him from the faithful, Bede) and appointing his portion "with the unbelievers" (better, the unfaithful, the untrustworthy, contrasted with the faithful steward, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson; "the hypocrites" in Matthew). The verse displays the certain and terrible judgment of the abuser of trust: presuming on delay (v.45), he is caught unawares by the sudden return of his Lord and condemned with the faithless. The reckoning is sure though the hour is unknown; and the servant once faithful, now turned to abuse, forfeits his place and shares the doom of the untrustworthy (Wesley). The verse warns every steward — especially the minister — that the abuse of trust ends not in mere demotion but in judgment with the faithless. The Lord who delays will come; and the unfaithful servant, caught in his wickedness, will be cut off and assigned his portion among the untrustworthy. The certainty of the reckoning is the ground of the warning.
47:
"And that servant, which knew his lord's will, and prepared not himself, neither did according to his will, shall be beaten with many stripes." The witnesses note the principle of proportionate judgment by knowledge. The fathers read the greater guilt of the knowing. Theophylact: "the greatness of his dignity shall the rather become a cause of his condemnation." Chrysostom: "greater knowledge is an occasion of greater punishment. Therefore shall the Priest, committing the same sin with the people, suffer a far heavier penalty."

The grammatical voices note the Jewish background. Lightfoot and Clarke: criminals were beaten with up to forty stripes (Deuteronomy 25:3), more for doubled crimes. Barnes: "They who have many privileges, who are often warned, who have the gospel, and do not repent... shall be far more severely punished than others." Ellicott notes the verses (47-50) are peculiar to Luke and "every word is full of profoundest interest," with "a latent reference to Judas."

The Reformed Calvin presses the lesson against the plea of ignorance: "in proportion as any man knowingly and willingly takes pleasure in despising the Lord, he deserves severer punishment... It is in vain for men to betake themselves to the plea of ignorance, in order to be freed from condemnation." Yet even ignorance does not wholly excuse (v.48). The knowing, deliberate transgressor incurs the heavier judgment.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the servant who knew his lord's will and did it not is beaten with many stripes — for greater knowledge is the occasion of greater punishment (Chrysostom), and the dignity of the knowing servant aggravates his guilt (Theophylact). The principle applies especially to ministers and to all who have the gospel: privilege and knowledge increase responsibility, and the deliberate, knowing transgressor incurs the heavier judgment (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin presses it against the plea of ignorance: those who knowingly despise the Lord deserve severer punishment, and ignorance is no adequate shield. The verse displays the justice of God in proportioning judgment to knowledge: the more light a man has, the more is required, and the greater his condemnation if he abuses it. The knowing servant, who understood his Lord's will yet neglected it, is most severely punished — a solemn warning to all who enjoy the privileges of the gospel. To know the will of God and not do it is the heavier guilt; and the servant most enlightened, if unfaithful, is most deserving of stripes.
48:
"But he that knew not, and did commit things worthy of stripes, shall be beaten with few stripes. For unto whomsoever much is given, of him shall be much required: and to whom men have committed much, of him they will ask the more." The witnesses note the milder judgment of the ignorant and the principle of proportionate responsibility. On the "few stripes," Ellicott reads "the tenderness of a considerate equity... Man's knowledge is the measure of his responsibilities," and finds in it "a gleam of hope on the darkness that lies behind the veil," the "tolerabilior damnatio of Augustine" — though "we know not whether the few stripes imply limited duration or suffering less acute."

Basil (under v.47) guards against universalism: "what is here said assigns neither measure nor end of punishments, but their differences. For a man may deserve unquenchable fire to either a slight or more intense degree." The "few stripes" mark degrees, not a limit, of judgment. Yet the ignorant are not wholly excused: Theophylact, "when he might have known, he would not, but being himself slothful, was the cause of his own ignorance."

On "much is given... much required," the Reformed Calvin draws the lesson: "the more eminent a man is, he ought to consider that so much the more has been entrusted to him... if he does not, like a field cultivated at greater expense, yield to the Lord more abundant produce, the abuse of that grace will cost him dear." Ellicott distinguishes the two clauses: gifts (general moral responsibility) and that "committed as a trust or deposit." The more given, the more required.

The witnesses agree: the servant who knew not is beaten with few stripes — a milder judgment proportioned to his lesser knowledge (Ellicott's "considerate equity") — yet not wholly excused, for his ignorance was often culpable (Theophylact). The "few stripes" mark degrees, not the limit or end, of punishment (Basil, against universalism); whether they imply lighter or shorter suffering, the witnesses leave to God (Ellicott). The governing principle is "to whom much is given, much required": responsibility is proportioned to privilege and trust (Calvin, Ellicott). The verse displays the perfect justice of God, who judges each according to his light: the knowing more severely (v.47), the ignorant more mildly, yet all according to truth. The gifts and trusts a man receives are the measure of his accountability; and the more God has given — in knowledge, opportunity, or office — the more he will require. The verse both comforts (in God's equity toward the less privileged) and warns (the privileged are most accountable). To whom much is given, much is required: a solemn word to all who enjoy the abundant light of the gospel.
49:
"I am come to send fire on the earth; and what will I, if it be already kindled?" The witnesses note this abrupt, intense utterance and debate the meaning of the "fire." The grammatical voices canvass the options. Gill: "either the Gospel, which is as fire... or zeal for it... or rather persecution... unless the Holy Ghost... should be meant." Barnes: "Fire here is the emblem of discord and contention... the result of my coming will be that there will be divisions." Ellicott: "the fire of judgment which shall burn up the chaff... the baptism of fire which shall purify and cleanse as well as destroy." Robertson: "Christ came to set the world on fire, and the conflagration had already begun" (Plummer) — "the very passion in Christ's heart."

On "what will I if it be already kindled," the witnesses note the difficult phrase. Lightfoot: "What will I is the same with This I will... the meaning is, This I will, that it be already kindled." Robertson: "How I wish that it were already kindled." Barnes: "what would I, but that it were kindled... I am even desirous that they should come." The phrase expresses Christ's intense desire that the purifying, dividing work of his coming be accomplished.

The Reformed Calvin reads it of the gospel's transforming power: "the gospel is metaphorically compared to fire, because it violently changes the face of things... when great commotions shall already begin to kindle, this is so far from being a reason why you should tremble, that it is rather a ground of strong confidence." He notes (with Robertson) this was "one of Christ's latest discourses," out of chronological place.

The witnesses agree on the substance — Christ came to cast fire on the earth, and longs for its kindling — while differing on what the fire signifies: the gospel that transforms (Calvin), the zeal and division it provokes (Barnes), the persecution it brings, the Spirit, or the fire of judgment that purifies and destroys (Ellicott). The difficult clause expresses Christ's intense desire that this fiery work be accomplished (Lightfoot, Robertson, Barnes: "how I wish it were already kindled"). The verse displays the passion in Christ's heart for the transforming, dividing, purifying effect of his coming — a fire already beginning to burn. Whatever the precise referent, the fire is the searching, dividing power of the gospel and the judgment it brings, which Christ came to cast on the earth and longs to see kindled. The verse, one of Christ's most intense utterances, reveals the fervour of his mission: not a comfortable peace but a consuming fire, which he desires to see ablaze. The Cross (v.50) is the kindling of this fire; and Christ, far from shrinking, longs for its accomplishment.
50:
"But I have a baptism to be baptized with; and how am I straitened till it be accomplished!" The witnesses note Christ's baptism of suffering and his intense constraint. The witnesses identify the baptism. Gill: "not water baptism... but the baptism of his sufferings is meant, which are compared to a baptism, because of the largeness and abundance of them; he was as it were immersed, or plunged into them." Robertson: "Christ will call his baptism the baptism of blood... The world is lit up with flames and Christ is bathed in blood" (Plummer). Ellicott: "the baptism of one who is come into deep waters, so that the floods pass over him."

On "how am I straitened," the grammatical voices note the intense feeling. Robertson: "synechomai... Paul uses it of his desire for death just as Jesus does here. The urge of the Cross is upon Jesus... We catch a glimpse of the tremendous passion in his soul that drove him on." Barnes: "How do I earnestly desire that it were passed!" The constraint is the pressure of the coming passion on Christ's human soul, both dreaded and desired.

The Reformed Calvin reads it of Christ's consecrating death: "there remains nothing but his last act, that by his death he may consecrate the renovation of the world... He compares death to a baptism, because the children of God, after having been immersed for a time by the death of the body, shortly afterwards rise again." Christ is straitened, eagerly pressing toward the Cross that must accomplish the world's renewal.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the "baptism" is Christ's coming passion and death — an immersion in suffering, a being plunged into deep waters, bathed in blood (Gill, Robertson, Ellicott). The "how am I straitened" expresses the intense constraint and pressure of the coming Cross on Christ's human soul, both dreaded and longed for (Robertson, Barnes). Calvin reads the baptism as the consecrating death by which Christ would renew the world. The verse displays the passion in Christ's heart as he presses toward the Cross: not shrinking but straitened, eager for the accomplishment of the suffering that must kindle the fire (v.49) and redeem the world. The metaphor shifts from fire (its effect on the world) to baptism (its cost to Christ himself): the world set ablaze, Christ bathed in blood. The verse reveals the inner urgency of Christ's mission — the tremendous passion that drove him toward the Cross, the constraint of a soul pressing through dread toward the redeeming death. Christ longs for his baptism of suffering to be accomplished, for by it the world's renewal is consecrated. The Cross is both his agony and his desire.
51:
"Suppose ye that I am come to give peace on earth? I tell you, Nay; but rather division." The witnesses note Christ's startling word that he brings division, not outward peace. Gill: "Christ came to make peace with God for men, and to give the Gospel of peace, and spiritual and eternal peace to men; but not external peace, especially that, which is not consistent with the preservation of truth." Robertson: "Peace at any price is not the purpose of Christ. It is a pity for family jars to come, but loyalty to Christ counts more than all else."

The Reformed Calvin resolves the apparent contradiction with the prophets' promises of peace: "as Christ is called our peace, and as the Gospel reconciles us to God, it follows that he also establishes a brotherly harmony amongst us... But that which the prophets describe is associated with faith, and has no existence but among the sincere worshippers of God... To unbelievers it does not come, though it is offered to them; nay, they cannot endure to be reconciled to God: and the consequence is, that the message of peace excites in them a greater tumult than before." Christ, "properly speaking, the author of peace, becomes the occasion of disturbances in consequence of the wickedness of men."

The division the witnesses read as the inevitable effect of the gospel in a divided world. Where some believe and others reject, the gospel that is peace to the believer becomes the occasion of conflict — even within families (vv.52-53). Christ warns his followers to expect not universal peace but opposition and division for the truth's sake.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ came to bring not outward peace but division — for though he is the author of peace with God and spiritual peace to believers (Gill), the gospel, where some believe and others reject, inevitably produces conflict. Calvin resolves the tension with the prophets: the peace they promised belongs to the godly and the conscience, but to unbelievers, who cannot endure reconciliation with God, the message of peace excites greater tumult; so Christ, properly the author of peace, becomes the occasion of division through human wickedness. Loyalty to Christ counts more than family harmony (Robertson). The verse displays the dividing power of the gospel: it brings true peace to those who receive it, but conflict where it is opposed. Christ warns his followers not to expect universal peace but to be ready for opposition, even within their own households (vv.52-53), for the truth divides the believing from the unbelieving. The "division" is not Christ's purpose but the effect of the world's enmity to the gospel of peace. To follow Christ is to choose his peace over the world's, even at the cost of division.
52:
"For from henceforth there shall be five in one house divided, three against two, and two against three." The brief verse details the division the gospel brings into households. Gill explains the five: "the father and the son, the mother and the daughter, and her daughter-in-law... now from the time of Christ's saying these words, or quickly after... when the Gospel should be preached more publicly and extensively, this should be the effect of it." The household of five is split, three against two.

Wesley draws the cause: "There being an irreconcilable enmity between the Spirit of Christ and the spirit of the world." The division within the very household arises because some receive Christ and others reject him; the gospel draws a line through the closest natural ties.

The division the witnesses read as the painful but inevitable effect of the gospel's coming. The household, naturally united, is split by the differing response to Christ: those who believe are set against those who reject, and the closest relations become divided. The verse particularizes the "division" of v.51, showing how it reaches into the family itself.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the division the gospel brings reaches into the household itself — five divided, three against two — as some receive Christ and others reject him (Gill). The cause is "the irreconcilable enmity between the Spirit of Christ and the spirit of the world" (Wesley): the gospel draws a line through the closest natural ties, setting believing members against unbelieving. The verse particularizes the "division" of v.51, showing its painful penetration into the family. The household, naturally one, is split by the differing response to Christ; and the believer must sometimes choose Christ over the unity of his own home. The verse warns Christ's followers that loyalty to him may cost them peace even with their nearest relations — for the gospel, received by some and rejected by others, divides the very household. The division is not Christ's aim but the effect of the world's enmity meeting the gospel's call; and the believer must be ready to bear even this cost for the truth's sake. Where the Spirit of Christ and the spirit of the world meet in one house, division follows; and the disciple must hold to Christ though it divide him from his own.
53:
"The father shall be divided against the son, and the son against the father; the mother against the daughter, and the daughter against the mother; the mother in law against her daughter in law, and the daughter in law against her mother in law." The brief verse completes the picture of household division. Gill: "Shall oppose him, differ with him, and be alienated from him, and so the other relations." The division is mutual and reaches every relation — parent against child, child against parent.

Wesley draws the cause and the present fulfilment: "For those who reject me will be implacable toward their very nearest relations who receive me. At this day also is this scripture fulfilled. Now likewise there is no concord between Christ and Belial." Barnes notes the allusion to Micah 7:6, the prophecy of such division.

The mutual division the witnesses read as the deepest penetration of the gospel's dividing effect. Not only are households split into factions (v.52), but the closest individual bonds — parent and child, the relations of the home — are set against one another by the differing response to Christ. The verse, echoing Micah 7:6, shows the gospel reaching into the most intimate ties.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of this section, completing the picture of division. The witnesses agree: the division reaches every household relation — father and son, mother and daughter, mother-in-law and daughter-in-law set against one another (Gill), echoing Micah 7:6 (Barnes). The cause is the implacable enmity of those who reject Christ toward their nearest who receive him; "at this day also is this scripture fulfilled" (Wesley). The verse displays the deepest penetration of the gospel's dividing effect: not merely households into factions but the closest individual bonds — parent and child, the intimate relations of the home — are split by the differing response to Christ. The gospel, received by some and rejected by others, sets even the nearest kin against one another. The verse warns the disciple that following Christ may divide him from father, mother, child, or kin — for there is "no concord between Christ and Belial" (Wesley). Yet the division is not Christ's aim but the effect of unbelieving enmity meeting the gospel's call; and the believer must hold to Christ though it cost him the dearest of natural ties. To love Christ above father and mother is the cost the gospel sometimes exacts; and the divided household is the price of loyalty to the dividing, saving truth.
54:
"And he said also to the people, When ye see a cloud rise out of the west, straightway ye say, There cometh a shower; and so it is." The witnesses note Christ's turning to the multitude with the lesson of the signs of the times. The grammatical voices explain the weather-signs. Barnes: the cloud rising out of the west, over the Mediterranean, brought rain; the people read the sky accurately. The fathers (per the Catena) read the natural signs as figures of the spiritual discernment the people lacked.

The point the witnesses read as the contrast between the people's skill in reading the weather and their blindness to the signs of the Messiah's coming. They could infer the shower from the western cloud and the heat from the south wind (v.55), yet could not (or would not) discern that the time of the Messiah had come, attested by his miracles and the prophets' predictions.

The weather-lore the witnesses note as drawn from common experience to convict the people of willful blindness. The same observation that read the sky should have read the signs of the time — the works and words of Christ, the fulfilment of prophecy — and concluded that the kingdom was at hand. Their failure was not of ability but of will.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the lesson of the signs of the times. The witnesses agree: Christ, turning to the multitude, draws on their skill in reading the weather — the western cloud foretelling a shower (Barnes) — to convict them of blindness to the signs of his coming. The people could infer the shower from the cloud and the heat from the south wind (v.55), reading the sky accurately; yet they could not, or would not, discern that the time of the Messiah had come, attested by his miracles and the prophets. The verse displays the contrast between natural shrewdness and spiritual blindness: the same observation that read the weather should have read the signs of the time and concluded that the kingdom was at hand. Their failure was not of ability but of will — a willful blindness that Christ calls hypocrisy (v.56). The verse opens the rebuke of those who, skilled in earthly discernment, are blind to the heavenly: who read the face of the sky but not the face of the time. The signs of Christ's coming were as plain to faith as the cloud to the eye; and the failure to discern them was inexcusable.
55:
"And when ye see the south wind blow, ye say, There will be heat; and it cometh to pass." The brief verse adds the second weather-sign. Barnes: "To the south and southwest of Judea were situated Arabia, Egypt, and Ethiopia, all warm or hot regions, and consequently the air that came from those quarters was greatly heated." Ellicott: the word for "heat" is "probably the burning wind, the simoom, which, blowing over the desert, scorched and withered all that was green."

The point the witnesses read as reinforcing v.54: the people read the south wind as surely as the western cloud, inferring heat from the one as rain from the other. Their skill in weather-lore was exact and habitual — "and it cometh to pass." Yet this very skill condemned their blindness to the signs of the time.

The two signs together (cloud and wind) the witnesses note as establishing the people's competence in discernment, which makes their spiritual blindness inexcusable. Able to read the sky with precision, they should have read the plainer signs of the Messiah's presence — his miracles, his teaching, the fulfilment of prophecy — and known that the kingdom was at hand.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of this section. The witnesses agree: the south wind, blowing from the hot regions of Arabia and Egypt (the scorching simoom, Ellicott), surely brings heat, as the western cloud brings rain (v.54). The people read both signs with exact, habitual skill — "and it cometh to pass" (Barnes). The two weather-signs together establish their competence in discernment, which makes their blindness to the signs of the time inexcusable. The verse reinforces the rebuke: those so skilled in reading the sky should have read the plainer signs of the Messiah's coming — his works, his words, the fulfilment of prophecy — and concluded that the kingdom was at hand. Their failure was not of ability but of will. The verse displays the indictment of willful blindness: men quick to read the weather, yet blind to the works of God before their eyes. The same shrewdness that prophesied heat from the south wind should have discerned the day of visitation; and the failure to do so was the hypocrisy Christ rebukes (v.56). To read the sky and not the time is the mark of a heart unwilling to see.
56:
"Ye hypocrites, ye can discern the face of the sky and of the earth; but how is it that ye do not discern this time?" The witnesses note Christ's sharp rebuke of the people's willful blindness. Gill notes the word "hypocrites" — "often used of the Scribes and Pharisees... which suggests there were such in company." Robertson: "No wonder that Jesus here calls them hypocrites because of their blindness when looking at and hearing him. So it is today with those who are willfully blind to the steps of God among men. This ignorance of the signs of the times is colossal."

On "this time," Ellicott: "It was the time of their visitation (Luke 19:44), and yet they knew it not." Barnes: "You see my miracles; you hear my preaching; you have the predictions of me in the prophets; why do you not, in like manner, infer that this is the time when the Messiah should appear?" The "time" is the season of the Messiah's presence, the crisis of their visitation, which they failed to discern.

The rebuke "hypocrites" the witnesses read as exposing the willfulness of their blindness. Their failure was not inability — they could read the sky — but unwillingness to read the plainer signs of God's working among them. Hence Christ calls them hypocrites: pretending an ignorance they need not have, refusing the evidence before their eyes.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ sharply rebukes the people as "hypocrites" for their willful blindness — able to discern the face of sky and earth, yet refusing to discern "this time," the season of the Messiah's presence and their visitation (Ellicott, Luke 19:44). Their failure was not of ability but of will (Robertson): they could read the weather but would not read the plainer signs of God's working — Christ's miracles, his preaching, the prophets' predictions (Barnes). Hence the charge of hypocrisy: feigning an ignorance they need not have, refusing the evidence before their eyes. The verse displays Christ's indictment of willful spiritual blindness: men sharp in earthly discernment but blind to the heavenly, who read the sky but not the time of their visitation. The "signs of the times" — the works and words of Christ fulfilling prophecy — were as plain to faith as the cloud to the eye; and the failure to discern them was inexcusable, a hypocrisy that Christ exposes. The verse warns every age against the willful blindness that, skilled in worldly things, refuses to discern the working of God. To read the sky and miss the Saviour is the colossal blindness of the hypocrite.
57:
"Yea, and why even of yourselves judge ye not what is right?" The witnesses note Christ's appeal to the people's own conscience and reason. Gill: "From their own observation, as in discerning the signs of the weather; in a rational way, by the light of reason... by what they themselves saw and heard; by the signs and wonders which were done, they might have concluded, that now was the time of the Messiah's coming." Robertson: "Without the presence and teaching of Jesus they had light enough to tell what is right and so without excuse as Paul argued in Romans 1-3."

Ellicott clarifies the sense: "the meaning is not that they did not know what was right, but that they did not act upon their knowledge. They were passing an unrighteous judgment on the preachers of repentance, on the Baptist and on the Christ... when their action ought to have been as true and spontaneous as their daily judgment about the weather."

The Reformed Calvin reads it as exposing the root of their blindness: "they do not descend into their consciences, and there examine with themselves, as in the presence of God, what is right. The reason why hypocrites are so much disposed to make objections is, that they throw their swelling words into the air without any concern, and never exercise calm thought, or place themselves at the tribunal of God, that the truth, when once ascertained, may be fully embraced."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ appeals to the people's own conscience and reason — "why even of yourselves judge ye not what is right?" They had light enough, by reason and observation, to discern the time of the Messiah and to judge rightly of Christ and the Baptist (Gill, Robertson, Romans 1-3); their fault was not lack of knowledge but failure to act on it (Ellicott). Calvin exposes the root: the hypocrites do not descend into their consciences to examine, in God's presence, what is right, but throw out objections without calm thought or submission to the truth. The verse displays Christ's appeal to the inner witness of conscience: the people had, of themselves, sufficient light to judge rightly, and were without excuse. Their unrighteous judgment of Christ and his forerunner was a refusal to act on the truth their own reason and conscience could discern. The verse calls every hearer to honest self-examination before God: to judge what is right not by prejudice or party but by the calm verdict of conscience enlightened by truth. To refuse this is the willful blindness of the hypocrite; to embrace it is the way of faith. The light is within; the fault is in not heeding it.
58:
"When thou goest with thine adversary to the magistrate, as thou art in the way, give diligence that thou mayest be delivered from him; lest he hale thee to the judge, and the judge deliver thee to the officer, and the officer cast thee into prison." The witnesses note the counsel to be reconciled with one's adversary, both literally and allegorically. The grammatical voices read the literal sense (from the Sermon on the Mount, Matthew 5:25). Clarke: "do every thing in thy power to get free before a suit commences." Ellicott: "the adversary is the Law that accuses them (John 5:45); the judge is none other than the Judge of all the earth," urging reconciliation before judgment.

The fathers read rich allegories. Ambrose: "our adversary is the devil... our adversary is also every vicious practice... our adversary is an evil conscience, which affects us both in this world, and will accuse us in the next. Let us then give heed, while we are in this life's course, that we may be delivered." Bede: "our adversary in the way is the word of God, which opposes our carnal desires in this life; from which he is delivered who is subject to its precepts." Origen reads the adversary as the accusing angel or the devil who rejoices in our sin.

Chrysostom notes the practical lesson drawn from the present: "by these things which are visible and at hand, ignorant men are wont to gain improvement." The counsel, whether of literal lawsuits or of the soul's reckoning with God, urges speedy reconciliation before judgment falls.

The witnesses agree: Christ counsels reconciliation with one's adversary before judgment — literally, prudence in settling lawsuits before they reach the magistrate (Matthew 5:25, Clarke), and allegorically, the urgent reckoning of the soul with God before death and judgment. The fathers read the adversary variously: the devil, an evil conscience, every vice, or the word of God that opposes our carnal desires (Ambrose, Bede, Origen); the judge as the Judge of all; the prison as hell. The lesson, drawn from the visible to instruct the ignorant (Chrysostom), is the urgency of reconciliation: as the wise debtor settles before the court, so the soul must be reconciled to God — by repentance, by submission to his word, by a cleansed conscience — before the day of judgment, lest it be cast into the prison from which (v.59) there is no release till the last is paid. The verse displays the urgency of repentance: now, "in the way," while there is time, the soul must make peace with God, lest judgment overtake it. The way of this life is the only season of reconciliation; and the wise will not delay.
59:
"I tell thee, thou shalt not depart thence, till thou hast paid the very last mite." The witnesses note the solemn conclusion — no release from the prison until the last debt is paid. The grammatical voices note the smallest coin. Ellicott: "St. Luke substitutes the yet smaller coin, the mite, or half-quadrans, for the farthing of St. Matthew." The phrase stresses the completeness and rigour of the reckoning: not the least is remitted.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke draws the lesson against any payment by suffering: "when can this be, if we understand the text spiritually? Can weeping, wailing, and gnashing of teeth, pay to Divine justice the debt a sinner has contracted? This is impossible." He turns to the urgency of present knowledge: "God will judge them for the knowledge they might have received... the plea of ignorance is a mere refuge of lies, and none can plead it who has the book of God within his reach."

The fathers (under v.58) read the "last mite" variously. Origen: the slight sins are quickly paid, but "he who has been guilty of a great debt will have endless ages for his payment." Bede: in the prison of hell, "because he will ever have to pay the penalty by suffering, but never by paying it obtain pardon, he will never come out." The phrase, whether read of graduated temporal punishment or of endless judgment, stresses the rigour of the reckoning.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the chapter. The witnesses agree: the conclusion stresses the rigour and completeness of the reckoning — no release from the prison until the very last mite (the smallest coin, Luke's even smaller than Matthew's, Ellicott) is paid. The verse warns of the inexorable justice that awaits the unreconciled. The fathers read the "last mite" variously: graduated payment (the slight debt quickly, the great over endless ages, Origen), or the endless prison of hell where the penalty is ever paid but pardon never obtained (Bede). Clarke denies that suffering can ever pay the debt to divine justice, and presses the urgency of present reconciliation, since none can plead ignorance who has God's word within reach. The verse displays the solemn end of the unreconciled soul: cast into the prison of judgment, it cannot depart till the uttermost is paid — and the debt of sin, the witnesses note, is one that suffering can never discharge. The lesson is the urgency of repentance now (v.58), "in the way," before judgment falls; for once cast into that prison, there is no release. The very last mite must be paid; and the wise will be reconciled to God while there is yet time, lest they fall under the rigour of a justice that remits nothing.
